title: A Complex-Valued Continuous-Variable Quantum Approximation Optimization Algorithm (CCV-QAOA)
summary: Continuous-variable (CV) quantum systems offer a natural framework for continuous optimization through their infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces. In this paper, we propose the Complex Continuous-Variable Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (CCV-QAOA), a variational framework operating in the complex domain that optimizes over complex decision variables. The method efficiently solves real and complex multivariate optimization problems. To demonstrate its versatility, we apply CCV-QAOA across a broad suite of optimization use cases, including convex quadratic minimization, scaling studies with circuit depth and cutoff dimension, constrained quadratic programs using penalty constructions, and non-convex benchmarks such as the Styblinski-Tang function and complex quartic landscapes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CCV-QAOA, a variational quantum algorithm for continuous optimization that operates on complex decision variables, and demonstrates its effectiveness across convex, constrained, and non-convex optimization problems.
AI.motivation: Continuous-variable quantum systems are naturally suited for continuous optimization due to infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, motivating the development of a variational algorithm that can handle both real and complex multivariate optimization problems.
AI.method: The Complex Continuous-Variable Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (CCV-QAOA) is a variational framework operating in the complex domain, using continuous-variable quantum systems to optimize over complex decision variables.
AI.result: CCV-QAOA is validated across convex quadratic minimization, scaling studies with circuit depth and cutoff dimension, constrained quadratic programs via penalty methods, and non-convex benchmarks including the Styblinski-Tang function and complex quartic landscapes.
AI.conclusion: CCV-QAOA is a versatile variational quantum algorithm that effectively solves a broad range of continuous optimization problems in both real and complex domains.

title: Diffusion with conserved marginal distributions and information theory in fracton hydrodynamics
summary: Diffusion with multipole-moment conservation gives rise to transport laws that generalize Fick's law and has attracted growing attention following experimental advances in strongly tilted optical lattices. It was recently shown that conserving complete multipole-moment groups leads to subdiffusive dynamics governed by a nonlinear diffusion equation, raising the question of whether hydrodynamic equations would also be nonlinear when the conservation law is imposed only at the subsystem level. Here we show that subsystem symmetries generically produce nonlinear hydrodynamic equations with shear-only transport, in which any localization present in the initial marginal distributions is preserved at long times by the conservation of those marginals. A linear regime emerges only as a limiting case for small fluctuations around a uniform background. We derive the deterministic and fluctuating parts of the hydrodynamic equations in arbitrary dimensions and obtain the corresponding maximum-entropy equilibrium distributions under constrained marginals. We also show that marginal-conserving diffusion provides a concrete hydrodynamic realization of partial multipole-moment conservation, and we offer an information-theoretic interpretation in which total correlation decays monotonically even when pairwise mutual information does not.

AI.tldr: This paper studies hydrodynamic transport laws arising from subsystem symmetries (conservation of multipole moments at the subsystem level). It shows that such symmetries generically lead to nonlinear diffusion equations with shear-only transport, where localization in initial marginal distributions is preserved at long times. Linear behavior emerges only as a limiting case. The authors derive deterministic and fluctuating hydrodynamic equations in arbitrary dimensions, obtain maximum-entropy equilibrium distributions, and link this to partial multipole-moment conservation with an information-theoretic interpretation.
AI.motivation: Experimental advances in strongly tilted optical lattices have sparked interest in transport laws with multipole-moment conservation beyond Fick's law. Previous work showed that conserving complete multipole-moment groups leads to subdiffusive nonlinear dynamics, raising the question of whether subsystem-level conservation laws also produce nonlinear hydrodynamics.
AI.method: The authors analyze subsystem symmetries (conservation laws imposed at the subsystem level) to derive generic nonlinear hydrodynamic equations. They separate deterministic and fluctuating parts of the equations in arbitrary dimensions, compute maximum-entropy equilibrium distributions under constrained marginals, and connect the framework to partial multipole-moment conservation and information theory.
AI.result: Subsystem symmetries generically yield nonlinear hydrodynamic equations with shear-only transport. Initial localization in marginal distributions is preserved at long times. Linear dynamics emerges only as a limiting case for small fluctuations around a uniform background. Marginal-conserving diffusion realizes partial multipole-moment conservation hydrodynamically.
AI.conclusion: The work generalizes the understanding of transport under subsystem symmetries, showing nonlinearity is generic rather than exceptional. It also provides an information-theoretic interpretation where total correlation decays monotonically even when pairwise mutual information does not.

title: Violation of a Monty-Hall constraint on determinism using a single qutrit
summary: We present a simple inequality that distinguishes deterministic hidden-variable theories (local or nonlocal) from standard quantum mechanics, using a single three-level system. The protocol is inspired by the Monty Hall puzzle: a coherent "descarte" procedure followed by a projective measurement. In any deterministic theory that respects the Monty Hall condition (the descarte never eliminates the real state), the probability of obtaining a chosen state after the descarte is exactly $1/3$. In contrast, quantum mechanics predicts $1/6$, due to the preparation of coherent superpositions. The inequality is violated by quantum mechanics, demonstrating that determinism (even without locality assumptions) is incompatible with quantum coherence in sequential measurements. An experimental implementation with photonic qutrits is proposed.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a simple inequality based on the Monty Hall puzzle that distinguishes deterministic hidden-variable theories from quantum mechanics using a single qutrit. Quantum mechanics predicts a probability of 1/6 versus 1/3 for deterministic theories, proving that determinism alone (without locality) is incompatible with quantum coherence in sequential measurements.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that determinism (even without locality assumptions) is incompatible with quantum coherence, using a minimal single-system setup inspired by the Monty Hall puzzle.
AI.method: A "descarte" procedure (coherent operation inspired by Monty Hall) followed by a projective measurement on a single three-level quantum system (qutrit). A deterministic inequality is derived based on the Monty Hall condition that the descarte never eliminates the real state.
AI.result: Quantum mechanics predicts a probability of 1/6 for obtaining a chosen state, while any deterministic hidden-variable theory respecting the Monty Hall condition predicts exactly 1/3, violating the derived inequality.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic hidden-variable theories (local or nonlocal) are incompatible with quantum coherence in sequential measurements, as shown by a single qutrit without any locality assumptions.

title: Topological transitions in spin-ice induced by geometrical constraints
summary: We study the nearest-neighbor spin-ice model subjected to a magnetic field applied along the global [111] and [110] directions, focusing on the role of sample geometry in stabilizing topological phase transitions. While no Kasteleyn transition is expected for this field orientations in the thermodynamic limit, we show that constraining the transverse dimensions of the system qualitatively changes the behavior. For samples elongated along the field direction with finite transverse area, the divergence-free constraint quantizes the number of string excitations that can span the system. As a result, the magnetization evolves through a cascade of discrete transitions corresponding to the successive entry of individual strings. Using Monte Carlo simulations, we demonstrate that each transition is marked by sharp magnetization steps and peaks in the specific heat and susceptibility, whose amplitudes scale linearly with the system length. We complement the numerical results with an analytical treatment based on the entropy - energy balance on a system with reduced dimensionality, deriving the critical fields associated with each topological sector. In the isotropic limit these transitions merge into a smooth crossover, but for anisotropic samples they remain sharply resolved, illustrating an unconventional mechanism by which finite geometry stabilizes topological phase transitions in frustrated magnets.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that constraining the transverse dimensions of spin-ice samples under [111] and [110] magnetic fields leads to discrete magnetization steps from individual string entries, i.e., topological phase transitions stabilized by finite geometry, even though no Kasteleyn transition occurs in the thermodynamic limit.
AI.motivation: To investigate how sample geometry (finite transverse area) can stabilize topological phase transitions in spin ice under [111] and [110] fields, which normally do not exhibit Kasteleyn transitions in the thermodynamic limit.
AI.method: Monte Carlo simulations of the nearest-neighbor spin-ice model in [111] and [110] fields with constrained transverse dimensions; complemented by an analytical entropy-energy balance treatment for reduced dimensionality.
AI.result: Magnetization evolves via discrete transitions (cascade) as individual strings enter the system, with sharp magnetization steps and specific heat/susceptibility peaks whose amplitudes scale linearly with system length. In isotropic samples the transitions merge into a smooth crossover, but remain sharply resolved for anisotropic samples.
AI.conclusion: Finite geometry (transverse confinement) provides an unconventional mechanism to stabilize topological phase transitions in frustrated magnets, quantizing string excitations and producing discrete magnetization jumps.

title: Coherent Rollout Oracles for Finite-Horizon Sequential Decision Problems
comment: 10 pages + 1 page of references, submitted to QCE 2026
summary:
Coherent quantum rollout for sequential decision problems requires a unitary simulator: randomness must live in explicit quantum registers, and basis-state selectors must be mapped to actions reversibly. With branch-dependent valid actions, this mapping is totalized coherent rank-select over an entangled $N$-bit validity mask: return the position of the $r$-th valid bit, or a sentinel if $r$ is out of range.
  We give the first reversible-circuit complexity analysis of this primitive. For selector width $w = \lceil \log_2(N+1) \rceil$, rank-select admits an $O(Nw)$-gate low-ancilla bounded-span scan, proved gate-optimal in its model, and an $O(N\log w)$-gate low-ancilla blocked construction when long-range gates are available; across all bounded-fan-in layouts, the unconditional gate lower bound is $Ω(N)$. Composing rank-select with reversible transition and predicate-evaluation circuits gives an explicit polynomial-size coherent rollout oracle for finite-horizon planning problems satisfying these primitive assumptions.
  The resulting oracle satisfies the access model of the best-arm pipeline of Wang et al., yielding $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{k}/\varepsilon)$ coherent oracle calls against the standard classical $Ω(k/\varepsilon^2)$ arm-pull lower bound. We give a bounded-influence lifting theorem that extends this lower-bound construction from a base configuration to an exponential family of configurations. We instantiate the construction on SIR epidemic intervention, with a stochastic placement-game sanity check, and machine-check the main results in Lean 4. Code and proofs: https://github.com/BinRoot/b01t/tree/main/demos/rollout.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first reversible-circuit complexity analysis of rank-select, a primitive for coherent quantum rollout in sequential decision problems. It provides gate-optimal constructions (\(O(Nw)\) scan, \(O(N\log w)\) blocked), proves an \(\Omega(N)\) unconditional lower bound, and composes these into a polynomial-size coherent rollout oracle. This yields \(\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{k}/\varepsilon)\) coherent oracle calls, improving over the classical \(\Omega(k/\varepsilon^2)\) bound. Results are instantiated on SIR epidemic intervention and machine-checked in Lean 4.
AI.motivation: Quantum algorithms for sequential decision problems (e.g., reinforcement learning, planning) require a coherent rollout oracle that simulates decision trajectories unitarily. A key subproblem is mapping branch-dependent valid actions to reversible actions via rank-select over an entangled validity mask. Prior work lacked reversible-circuit complexity analysis of this primitive, motivating the need for gate-optimal constructions and lower bounds.
AI.method: The paper defines rank-select over an \(N\)-bit validity mask with selector width \(w = \lceil \log_2(N+1)\rceil\). It proposes two constructions: (1) an \(O(Nw)\)-gate low-ancilla bounded-span scan (proved gate-optimal in its model), and (2) an \(O(N\log w)\)-gate low-ancilla blocked construction using long-range gates. It proves an unconditional \(\Omega(N)\) gate lower bound across all bounded-fan-in layouts. These are composed with reversible transition and predicate-evaluation circuits to build a coherent rollout oracle. The oracle is plugged into the best-arm pipeline of Wang et al., achieving \(\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{k}/\varepsilon)\) calls. A bounded-influence lifting theorem extends lower bounds to exponential families. The construction is instantiated on SIR epidemic intervention with a stochastic placement-game check, and results are machine-checked in Lean 4.
AI.result:
- First reversible-circuit complexity analysis of rank-select, with gate-optimal \(O(Nw)\) scan and \(O(N\log w)\) blocked constructions.
- Unconditional \(\Omega(N)\) gate lower bound for bounded-fan-in layouts.
- Polynomial-size coherent rollout oracle for finite-horizon planning.
- \(\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{k}/\varepsilon)\) coherent oracle calls vs. classical \(\Omega(k/\varepsilon^2)\) lower bound.
- Bounded-influence lifting theorem extending lower bounds to exponential families.
- Lean 4 machine-checked implementation with SIR epidemic intervention case study.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes reversible-circuit foundations for coherent rollout, demonstrating provably optimal constructions for rank-select and showing asymptotic quantum advantage in oracle complexity for sequential decision problems. The Lean 4 verification and SIR intervention instantiation validate practical feasibility.

title: Complex first-passage transport in ring networks with long-range jumps and stochastic resetting
comment: 3 pages, 9 figures. Analytical and numerical study of MFPT and transport in ring networks with long-range shortcuts and stochastic resetting. Submitted
summary: The transport properties of discrete-time random walks on ring networks with deterministic shortcuts are investigated through analytical and numerical methods. The network consists of a periodic chain where each node is connected to its nearest neighbors and to nodes located at a fixed distance $r$. Using the spectral properties of the transition matrix, we derive explicit expressions for the occupation probabilities and mean first-passage times (MFPTs). Contrary to the common expectation that shortcuts monotonically enhance transport, we find that the MFPT between distant nodes develops a highly non-monotonic dependence on the shortcut length. Beyond a threshold value, the MFPT landscape exhibits a hierarchy of maxima and minima organized in a self-similar pattern associated with commensurability relations between the shortcut length and the system size. The scaling behavior of these extrema reveals regimes where transport efficiency is either strongly enhanced or suppressed. We further analyze the mean squared displacement and the influence of stochastic resetting, showing that resetting amplifies the oscillatory MFPT structure and induces strongly nonuniform stationary distributions. These results demonstrate that the spatial organization of long-range connections plays a crucial role in determining transport efficiency in networks.

AI.tldr: This paper studies discrete-time random walks on ring networks with deterministic shortcuts, finding that mean first-passage times exhibit a non-monotonic, self-similar dependence on shortcut length due to commensurability effects, with stochastic resetting amplifying these oscillations.
AI.motivation: To understand how the spatial organization of long-range connections (shortcuts) affects transport efficiency in networks, challenging the common assumption that shortcuts always enhance transport.
AI.method: Analytical and numerical methods using spectral properties of the transition matrix on a periodic ring network with nearest-neighbor connections and deterministic shortcuts of fixed distance r.
AI.result: MFPT between distant nodes shows non-monotonic, self-similar dependence on shortcut length with a hierarchy of maxima and minima due to commensurability; stochastic resetting amplifies oscillations and induces nonuniform stationary distributions.
AI.conclusion: Spatial organization of long-range connections critically determines transport efficiency; shortcuts can either enhance or suppress transport depending on length relative to system size.

title: A Lie-algebraic Criterion for the Universality of Exponentiated Quantum Gates
summary: We present a criterion that serves as the basis for a polynomial-time algorithm to decide whether a finite set of qudit gates exponentiated by some Hamiltonians is universal. Our approach formulates universality in Lie algebraic terms and applies Borel--de Siebenthal theory with a diagonal generator having incommensurate spectrum. In this framework, nonuniversality is detected by invariant subspaces, equivalently by a graph-connectivity obstruction, while universality is repaired by adding generators that couple disconnected components. We further prove that two generators are sufficient for universal control. Our work reveals a profound link between qudit universality and irreducibility of Lie algebra representations.

AI.tldr: A polynomial-time algorithm using Lie algebra and Borel-de Siebenthal theory determines qudit gate universality, with nonuniversality detected by graph-connectivity obstructions and universality repaired by coupling generators; two generators suffice for universal control.
AI.motivation: To develop a polynomial-time criterion and algorithm for deciding whether a finite set of qudit gates (exponentiated Hamiltonians) is universal, filling a gap in qudit universality theory.
AI.method: Formulates universality in Lie algebraic terms, applies Borel-de Siebenthal theory with a diagonal generator having incommensurate spectrum, detects nonuniversality via invariant subspaces/graph-connectivity obstructions, and repairs universality by adding coupling generators.
AI.result: A polynomial-time algorithm for universality decidability; proof that two generators suffice for universal control; identification of a link between qudit universality and irreducibility of Lie algebra representations.
AI.conclusion: Qudit universality is intimately connected to Lie algebra representation irreducibility, and the framework provides both detection of nonuniversality and systematic repair via generator addition.

title: Quo vadis, stochastic thermodynamics?
summary: Stochastic thermodynamics is a framework for describing non-equilibrium processes at the level of fluctuating trajectories, where the state of a system evolves as a stochastic time series, allowing thermodynamic quantities such as work, heat, and entropy production to be defined along individual realizations rather than at the ensemble level only. Over the past three decades, the field has yielded fundamental results, including fluctuation theorems and several universal bounds, such as thermodynamic uncertainty relations, speed limit theorems, and many others. Many of them have been tested on a range of experimental platforms. This Perspective reviews recent developments in stochastic thermodynamics that extend its scope beyond its traditional domains, including systems with memory and hidden degrees of freedom, microscopic approaches to interacting and active matter, and geometric formulations based on optimal transport. Next, the Perspective surveys the challenges that arise when applying these ideas to macroscopic and complex systems, where the link between statistical irreversibility and thermodynamic dissipation becomes less direct. Finally, emerging applications in non-physical contexts are highlighted, including computation, biological systems, and social dynamics. Transcending the traditional boundaries of physics, these developments catalyze an unorthodox framework to tackle the thermodynamics of complex systems.

AI.tldr: This Perspective reviews recent advances in stochastic thermodynamics beyond traditional domains, covering memory effects, hidden degrees of freedom, active/interacting matter, optimal transport formulations, and emerging applications in computation, biology, and social dynamics.
AI.motivation: To extend stochastic thermodynamics beyond its traditional non-equilibrium frameworks to address complex, macroscopic systems where the link between irreversibility and dissipation is less direct, and to explore non-physical applications.
AI.method: A Perspective review synthesizing developments in (1) systems with memory and hidden degrees of freedom, (2) microscopic approaches to interacting and active matter, (3) geometric formulations via optimal transport, and (4) applications to computation, biology, and social dynamics.
AI.result: Identification of key challenges in applying stochastic thermodynamics to macroscopic/complex systems; demonstration of universal bounds (fluctuation theorems, thermodynamic uncertainty relations, speed limits) across diverse platforms; emergence of a cross-disciplinary framework transcending physics.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic thermodynamics is evolving into an unorthodox, cross-disciplinary framework capable of tackling thermodynamics of complex systems beyond traditional physical boundaries.

title: No Tile Left Behind: Multiprogramming for Surface-Code Architectures
summary: Fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) is emerging as the architectural regime in which practical large-scale quantum workloads will execute. In this setting, however, multiprogramming is no longer a matter of partitioning a flat pool of qubits. Quantum error correction exposes a structured floorplan of data tiles, ancilla tiles, and magic-state service resources, so concurrent execution must account for compact placement, connectivity, routing headroom, and shared support infrastructure. This makes FTQC multiprogramming fundamentally harder than its NISQ counterpart: admission decisions can fragment the remaining floorplan, conservative reservations can waste ancilla, and dynamic contention across data, ancilla, and magic-state resources can degrade both throughput and quality of service. In this work, we develop a formal framework for FTQC multiprogramming that captures these structural constraints and their runtime implications. We formulate the baseline static allocation problem, extend it to limited-resource and online settings through hierarchy-aware scheduling policies, and further generalize it to cultivation-enabled architectures with dynamic magic-state generation. Through simulation on synthetic Clifford+T workloads, the proposed scheduler achieves a normalized system speedup of 3.1x, improving over prior FTQC multiprogramming baselines by ~29% while maintaining low mean slowdown.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a formal framework for multiprogramming in fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC), addressing the structural constraints of error-corrected floorplans (data tiles, ancilla tiles, magic-state resources). It formulates static allocation, extends to online/hierarchy-aware scheduling, and generalizes to cultivation-enabled architectures. The proposed scheduler achieves 3.1x normalized system speedup and ~29% improvement over prior FTQC baselines with low mean slowdown.
AI.motivation: FTQC multiprogramming is fundamentally harder than NISQ counterpart because quantum error correction exposes a structured floorplan with data tiles, ancilla tiles, and magic-state resources, making concurrent execution face challenges like floorplan fragmentation, wasted ancilla from conservative reservations, and dynamic resource contention degrading throughput and QoS.
AI.method: The authors develop a formal framework for FTQC multiprogramming capturing structural constraints and runtime implications. They formulate the baseline static allocation problem, extend it to limited-resource and online settings via hierarchy-aware scheduling policies, and further generalize to cultivation-enabled architectures with dynamic magic-state generation. Evaluation uses synthetic Clifford+T workloads.
AI.result: The proposed scheduler achieves a normalized system speedup of 3.1x, improving over prior FTQC multiprogramming baselines by ~29% while maintaining low mean slowdown.
AI.conclusion: The formal framework successfully addresses the unique challenges of FTQC multiprogramming—floorplan fragmentation, ancilla waste, resource contention—and demonstrates significant throughput improvement, establishing a foundation for practical concurrent execution in error-corrected quantum architectures.

title: Largest eigenvalue and top eigenvector statistics of large Euclidean random matrices
summary: Euclidean random matrices arise in a wide range of physical systems where interactions are determined by spatial configurations, including disordered media and cooperative phenomena in atomic ensembles. Unlike classical random matrix ensembles, their entries are strongly correlated through the geometry of the underlying random points, making their analytical treatment challenging. While global spectral properties such as the spectral density are relatively well understood, much less is known about extremal eigenvalues and the associated eigenvectors, despite their central role in applications. Here we address the problem of characterising the largest eigenvalue and the corresponding top eigenvector of large Euclidean random matrices with a quadratic kernel. For vectors in any dimension $d \geq 1$ drawn independently from a common distribution, we show that both quantities can be computed within a unified replica-based framework, leading to a set of self-consistent equations involving a small number of parameters. This approach yields an explicit expression for the average largest eigenvalue, fully determined by low-order moments of the underlying distribution, and an analytical characterisation of the distribution of top eigenvector's components in the large-$N$ limit. We find that the top eigenvector exhibits a non-trivial geometric structure, with components concentrating on a hypersurface determined by the same parameters controlling the largest eigenvalue. We further perform extensive numerical simulations that confirm these predictions. More broadly, our work provides a general framework to access extremal spectral properties of Euclidean random matrices.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a replica-based analytical framework to characterize the largest eigenvalue and top eigenvector of large Euclidean random matrices with quadratic kernels, deriving explicit expressions governed by low-order moments of the point distribution, and revealing non-trivial geometric concentration of eigenvector components on a hypersurface.
AI.motivation: Euclidean random matrices are challenging to analyze due to strong geometric correlations between entries. While global spectral properties are understood, extremal eigenvalues and eigenvectors—critical for applications—remain largely unexplored.
AI.method: The authors develop a unified replica-based framework that reduces the problem to self-consistent equations involving a small number of parameters, deriving an explicit expression for the average largest eigenvalue in terms of low-order moments and analytically characterizing top eigenvector components in the large-N limit.
AI.result: Explicit expression for the average largest eigenvalue determined solely by low-order moments; analytical characterization of the distribution of top eigenvector components; discovery that eigenvectors concentrate on a hypersurface governed by the same parameters; extensive numerical simulations confirm predictions.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a general framework for accessing extremal spectral properties of Euclidean random matrices, bridging a gap between known global spectral results and the practically important extremal regime.

title: Characterization of Thermalization Behaviour in a Generalized Aubry-André Model
summary: Although random matrix theory provides a fundamental framework for characterizing quantum chaos, encompassing both ergodic and localized phases, a comprehensive understanding of the universal features governing the critical transition remains elusive in many disordered and quasi-random systems. In this study, we explore the ergodic-to-many-body localization transition in the generalized Aubry-André model with interacting spinless fermions. Using the concept of Frobenius norm of an adiabatic gauge potential, we construct a phase diagram that captures the sensitivity of the eigenspectrum to infinitesimal adiabatic gauge deformations. To examine the stability of the critical disordered strength with respect to system size, we perform an unbiased finite-size scaling analysis via cost-function minimization techniques. Additionally, by analyzing the adjacent gap ratio and spectral form factor, we determine the scaling behavior of the Thouless time as a function of the disorder strength.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the ergodic-to-many-body localization transition in the generalized Aubry-André model with interacting spinless fermions, using the Frobenius norm of an adiabatic gauge potential to construct a phase diagram, finite-size scaling to examine critical disorder strength stability, and Thouless time scaling via gap ratio and spectral form factor analysis.
AI.motivation: Despite random matrix theory providing a framework for quantum chaos in ergodic and localized phases, the universal features governing the critical transition in disordered and quasi-random systems remain poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors use the Frobenius norm of an adiabatic gauge potential to construct a phase diagram, perform unbiased finite-size scaling via cost-function minimization, and analyze the adjacent gap ratio and spectral form factor to determine Thouless time scaling.
AI.result: A phase diagram capturing eigenspectrum sensitivity to adiabatic gauge deformations is constructed; the stability of critical disorder strength with system size is examined; scaling behavior of Thouless time as a function of disorder strength is determined.
AI.conclusion: The adiabatic gauge potential approach, combined with finite-size scaling and Thouless time analysis, provides a comprehensive characterization of the ergodic-to-MBL transition in quasi-random interacting systems.

title: Solvable Random Unitary Dynamics in a Disordered Tomonaga-Luttinger Liquid
comment: main text, 7 pages 4 figures. supplementary material 10 pages 3 figures
summary: Disordered one-dimensional interacting systems have long been characterized via conventional correlation functions. A complementary quantum-information perspective quantifies the randomness of the unitary ensemble dynamics generated by a quantum system through the frame potential, which serves as a practical diagnostic for quantum algorithmic performance. However, no analytical treatment has yet been achieved for experimentally accessible interacting one-dimensional systems. In this Letter, we derive a closed-form expression for the frame potential of a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid with quenched Gaussian forward-scattering disorder. Exploiting the exactly quadratic structure of the disorder-averaged Keldysh action, we show that the frame potential decays as a power law at early times and saturates to a late-time plateau controlled by a single coupling parameter. Taking the random field XXZ spin chain as a specific microscopic realization, we show that the strongest randomness is achieved near the Heisenberg ferromagnetic point and can be exponentially enhanced through a multiple-quench protocol. We validate our results across the entire gapless phase, with direct implications for algorithm design in analog quantum simulation platforms.

AI.tldr: This paper derives an analytical closed-form expression for the frame potential of a disordered Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid, showing power-law decay at early times and saturation to a plateau controlled by a single coupling parameter. Using the random field XXZ spin chain as a microscopic model, the strongest randomness is found near the Heisenberg ferromagnetic point and can be exponentially enhanced via multiple-quench protocols.
AI.motivation: To provide an analytical treatment of the frame potential—a quantum-information diagnostic for algorithmic performance—in experimentally accessible disordered interacting one-dimensional systems, which had previously lacked such analysis.
AI.method: Derives a closed-form expression for the frame potential of a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid with quenched Gaussian forward-scattering disorder by exploiting the exactly quadratic structure of the disorder-averaged Keldysh action. Validates the theory with the random field XXZ spin chain as a microscopic realization.
AI.result: The frame potential decays as a power law at early times and saturates to a late-time plateau governed by a single coupling parameter. The strongest randomness occurs near the Heisenberg ferromagnetic point and can be exponentially enhanced through a multiple-quench protocol.
AI.conclusion: The analytical results are validated across the entire gapless phase, with direct implications for algorithm design in analog quantum simulation platforms.

title: High-fidelity entangling gates and nonlocal circuits with neutral atoms
summary: Creation and manipulation of entanglement with low error is essential in quantum information systems. In practice, two-qubit entangling gates constitute a dominant error source, limiting circuit depths and performance in fault-tolerant architectures. Using a neutral-atom quantum processor, we realize entangling CZ gates with a high Rabi frequency smooth-amplitude pulse, employing state-selective readout and qubit reuse for fast calibration, and achieve state-of-the-art fidelities of 99.854(4)% which improve to 99.941(3)% upon loss postselection, with stable performance for 10 hours. We then use these low-error gates in quantum circuits with coherent atom rearrangement. We first benchmark performance by creating and disentangling cluster states, and subsequently implement scrambling circuits featuring longer-range connectivity to study non-locally entangled states generated through chaotic dynamics. These results pave the way towards deep-circuit, efficient fault-tolerant quantum computation.

AI.tldr: Achieves 99.854% (99.941% with loss postselection) fidelity CZ gates in a neutral-atom quantum processor using smooth-amplitude pulses, state-selective readout, and qubit reuse, with stable 10-hour performance; demonstrates cluster states and scrambling circuits with coherent atom rearrangement for non-local entanglement generation.
AI.motivation: Two-qubit entangling gates are a dominant error source in quantum information systems, limiting circuit depths and fault-tolerant performance. Reducing gate errors is essential for scalable quantum computation.
AI.method: Uses a neutral-atom quantum processor with high Rabi frequency smooth-amplitude pulses for entangling CZ gates, combined with state-selective readout and qubit reuse for fast calibration. Coherent atom rearrangement enables quantum circuits with varying connectivity.
AI.result: CZ gate fidelities of 99.854(4)% (99.941(3)% with loss postselection) with stable performance over 10 hours. Demonstrated via cluster state benchmarking and scrambling circuits with long-range connectivity.
AI.conclusion: These low-error gates and coherent rearrangement techniques pave the way toward deep-circuit, efficient fault-tolerant quantum computation.

title: Typical entanglement entropy with charge conservation
summary: We consider a many-body Hilbert space with a fixed global charge and show that the typical entanglement entropy of a subsystem, at the leading and subleading order in the thermodynamic limit, can be expressed in terms of a single quantity which represents the local thermal entropy at fixed charge density. We find a general formula which applies both to abelian U(1) symmetry and non-abelian SU(2) symmetry, including the case of a local Hilbert space which transforms under a general reducible representation of the symmetry group. We illustrate the general formula with model systems and discuss the relevance of the results as a probe of quantum chaos for physical Hamiltonians.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that the typical entanglement entropy of a subsystem in a many-body Hilbert space with fixed global charge can be expressed, at leading and subleading orders in the thermodynamic limit, in terms of the local thermal entropy at fixed charge density. The formula applies to both abelian U(1) and non-abelian SU(2) symmetries, including general reducible representations.
AI.motivation: To understand how global symmetry constraints (charge conservation) affect typical entanglement entropy in many-body systems, and to derive general analytic formulas that capture both leading and subleading behavior.
AI.method: The authors derive a general formula for typical entanglement entropy in Hilbert spaces with fixed global charge, using thermodynamic arguments and symmetry considerations. They apply the formalism to both abelian U(1) and non-abelian SU(2) symmetries, including cases where the local Hilbert space transforms under general reducible representations. The results are illustrated with model systems.
AI.result: The typical entanglement entropy at leading and subleading order in the thermodynamic limit is expressed solely in terms of the local thermal entropy at fixed charge density. The formula works for both abelian and non-abelian symmetries and for general reducible representations.
AI.conclusion: The results provide a universal link between entanglement entropy and thermal entropy under symmetry constraints, and serve as a probe of quantum chaos for physical Hamiltonians.

title: Arbitrary parallel entangling gates with independent calibration on a trapped ion quantum computer
summary: Parallel processing of information plays a critical role in accelerating computation. This includes quantum computers, where parallel processing of quantum information will play a critical role in practical quantum advantage. Here, we demonstrate a new type of parallel entangling gates in a trapped-ion quantum computer, that simultaneously provides efficient gate-pulse synthesis and calibration, as well as graph-pattern-agnostic implementation. We demonstrate the resulting reduced execution time in three well-known algorithms, exhibiting disjoint gates, a star graph and a ring graph respectively. For disjoint qubit pairs the execution time of our parallel gates is comparable to that of a single-pair entangling gate resulting in an approximately linear speed up. For all graph patterns our parallel gate fidelities are comparable to the fidelity of a single-pair entangling gate. These advantages motivate architectures featuring multiple medium length ion chains in future quantum computing devices.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates a new type of parallel entangling gates in a trapped-ion quantum computer that enables efficient gate-pulse synthesis, calibration, and graph-pattern-agnostic implementation, achieving execution time comparable to a single-pair gate for disjoint qubit pairs and maintaining comparable fidelities across various graph patterns, motivating architectures with multiple medium-length ion chains.
AI.motivation: Parallel processing in quantum computing is critical for accelerating computation and achieving practical quantum advantage, but existing approaches lack efficient parallel entangling gates that are pattern-agnostic and easy to calibrate.
AI.method: A new type of parallel entangling gates is designed and implemented on a trapped-ion quantum computer, featuring joint optimization of gate-pulse synthesis and calibration, and supporting arbitrary graph patterns (disjoint, star, ring) without pattern-specific tuning.
AI.result: Execution time for disjoint qubit pairs is comparable to a single-pair entangling gate, yielding approximately linear speedup. Fidelities for all graph patterns (disjoint pairs, star graph, ring graph) remain comparable to single-pair gate fidelities.
AI.conclusion: Parallel entangling gates with these properties motivate future quantum computing architectures based on multiple medium-length ion chains, offering scalable and efficient parallel quantum operations.

title: Over forty years of research towards the understanding of Quantum Brownian Motion -- the contributions of A. O. Caldeira
comment: Brief review submitted to the Special Issue "Frontiers in Quantum Mechanics and the 45 Years of the Caldeira-Leggett model" of the Brazilian Journal of Physics to celebrate the career of A. O. Caldeira
summary: This article presents a brief account of Amir O. Caldeira's contributions to the theory of quantum Brownian motion. Motivated by its importance, we outline the description of Brownian motion in the quantum regime following Caldeira's first works. In this context, we particularly highlight the effect of dissipation on the tunneling rate out of a metastable state. We then journey along the alternative ways to approach quantum Brownian motion developed by Caldeira during his career, which go beyond the so-called Caldeira-Leggett model. We conclude by summarizing some of Caldeira's contributions to contemporary fields such as the theory of quantum decoherence and quantum thermodynamics, that were strongly inspired by his eponymous approach to quantum Brownian motion.

AI.tldr: This article reviews Amir O. Caldeira's contributions to quantum Brownian motion theory, from the early Caldeira-Leggett model (focusing on dissipation effects on tunneling) to alternative approaches, and his influence on quantum decoherence and quantum thermodynamics.
AI.motivation: To honor and provide a historical account of Caldeira's foundational and lasting contributions to quantum Brownian motion, motivated by the importance of these ideas across multiple subfields of quantum physics.
AI.method: The paper presents a narrative review: starting with Caldeira's early work on dissipation in quantum tunneling (Caldeira-Leggett model), then surveying alternative approaches he later developed beyond that model, and finally connecting these to applications in quantum decoherence and thermodynamics.
AI.result: The paper highlights how Caldeira's work established the standard framework for quantum Brownian motion, particularly the role of dissipation in suppressing tunneling rates, and shows how his models informed later theories of decoherence and quantum thermodynamics.
AI.conclusion: Caldeira's eponymous approach to quantum Brownian motion has had a broad and lasting impact, inspiring key developments in quantum decoherence and quantum thermodynamics.

title: Reservoir-mediated spin entanglement in the mean-force Gibbs state
summary: Two qubits strongly coupled to a common bosonic reservoir can become entangled with each other, despite having no direct interaction. In equilibrium, such coupling-induced coherences can be described by the mean-force Gibbs state. Here we derive approximate, analytic expressions for the two-qubit mean-force Gibbs state, and use these to characterize equilibrium qubit-qubit entanglement mediated by a thermal reservoir. Entanglement, which is highest at lowest temperatures, is a non-monotonic function of the system-reservoir coupling strength. Moreover, we find that broadening the reservoir spectral density beyond a single mode, as is realistic for typical baths, can enhance the qubit entanglement. Our results provide a comprehensive understanding of reservoir-mediated two-qubit entanglement in thermal equilibrium and provide a benchmark to compare with numerical methods, as well as demonstrating the utility of strong system-reservoir coupling as a resource.

AI.tldr: This paper derives approximate analytic expressions for the two-qubit mean-force Gibbs state to characterize equilibrium qubit-qubit entanglement mediated by a thermal bosonic reservoir, finding that entanglement is highest at low temperatures, non-monotonic in system-reservoir coupling strength, and enhanced by broadening the reservoir spectral density beyond a single mode.
AI.motivation: To develop a comprehensive analytic understanding of reservoir-mediated two-qubit entanglement in thermal equilibrium, and to demonstrate that strong system-reservoir coupling can serve as a useful resource for generating entanglement.
AI.method: Derives approximate analytic expressions for the two-qubit mean-force Gibbs state (the equilibrium state of two qubits strongly coupled to a common bosonic reservoir without direct qubit-qubit interaction).
AI.result: (1) Entanglement is highest at lowest temperatures; (2) Entanglement is a non-monotonic function of system-reservoir coupling strength; (3) Broadening the reservoir spectral density beyond a single mode enhances qubit entanglement.
AI.conclusion: The results provide comprehensive understanding of reservoir-mediated two-qubit equilibrium entanglement, offer benchmarks for numerical methods, and demonstrate strong system-reservoir coupling as a useful resource.

title: Continuous Noise Model for Quantum Circuits
summary: Quantum noise is a central challenge in quantum computing across many applications. Extensive work has examined how qubits couple to their environment, leading to decoherence and relaxation, which is irreversible. Current studies focus on coherent gate errors caused by control misalignment, which accumulate with circuit depth but can, in principle, be corrected. This work studies a continuous coherent noise model for quantum circuits and compares it with a discrete Pauli model. The focus is on small coherent gate errors that build up across circuit depth. These errors are modeled as random rotations on the Bloch sphere using a von Mises-Fisher distribution. In the small-angle limit, the model reduces to an isotropic Gaussian distribution. We test the model on quantum error-correction circuits based on the [[5,1,3]] and [[7,1,3]] codes. A variant of Grover's search circuit with different qubit counts is also examined. To enable fair comparison, we introduce a model-independent matching scheme. Pauli and continuous noise channels are aligned using the binary entropy at readout. This isolates the effect of noise structure at fixed uncertainty. An approximate analytical method for coherent error propagation is also developed. The method tracks error distributions through Clifford circuits without full Monte Carlo sampling. It reduces simulation cost while preserving accuracy for circuit-level error estimates. The approximation is validated against brute-force simulations, identifying its regime of validity with Clifford circuits and limits under error correction. Our results show that continuous coherent noise can degrade logical performance more strongly than Pauli noise. They also clarify when simplified propagation models succeed and when they break down.

AI.tldr: This paper compares continuous coherent noise (modeled as small random rotations on the Bloch sphere) against discrete Pauli noise in quantum circuits, showing that coherent noise can degrade logical performance more severely. It introduces a model-independent matching scheme using binary entropy, an approximate analytical method for tracking coherent error propagation without full Monte Carlo sampling, and validates findings on [[5,1,3]] and [[7,1,3]] quantum error-correction codes and Grover's search circuits.
AI.motivation: Existing studies treat quantum noise as either decoherence/relaxation (irreversible) or coherent gate errors from control misalignment (correctable in principle). However, the impact of small, continuous coherent errors that accumulate across circuit depth is not well understood compared to standard Pauli noise models, motivating the need for a systematic comparison.
AI.method: The authors model continuous coherent errors as random rotations on the Bloch sphere using a von Mises-Fisher distribution (reducing to isotropic Gaussian in the small-angle limit). They introduce a model-independent matching scheme that aligns Pauli and continuous noise channels using binary entropy at readout, isolating the effect of noise structure at fixed uncertainty. They also develop an approximate analytical method for tracking coherent error propagation through Clifford circuits without full Monte Carlo sampling.
AI.result: Continuous coherent noise can degrade logical performance more strongly than Pauli noise. The approximate analytical method reduces simulation cost while preserving accuracy for circuit-level error estimates; its regime of validity and limits under error correction are identified via brute-force validation.
AI.conclusion: Coherent noise poses a greater threat to quantum error correction than Pauli noise, and simplified propagation models (like Pauli twirling approximations) have clear limits where they break down under error correction. The work provides both a matching scheme for fair comparison and a cost-efficient analytical propagation method.

title: A Gaussian asymmetry measure
summary: The study of Entanglement Asymmetry has emerged in recent years as a powerful tool to characterise the symmetry properties of quantum states in relation to a given charge operator through the lens of entanglement. While extremely powerful and general, the standard definition of asymmetry introduces significant non-Gaussian features in free-fermionic systems, leading to certain analytical limitations. In this work, we introduce an asymmetry measure that remains strictly within the Gaussian manifold and analyse its properties. In particular, we show that it quantifies the minimal distance between a Gaussian state and the manifold of symmetric Gaussian states. We further demonstrate that this measure captures the established dynamical signatures of entanglement asymmetry, such as the Mpemba effect, symmetry restoration, and the lack thereof. The Gaussian structure allows these novel asymmetry measures to be computed exactly using correlation matrix techniques, and to be described asymptotically through the quasiparticle picture. We also comment on the possibility of using charge fluctuations to characterise the asymmetry of a Gaussian state.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Gaussian asymmetry measure for free-fermionic systems that stays within the Gaussian manifold, quantifies the minimal distance to symmetric Gaussian states, captures dynamical signatures like the Mpemba effect and symmetry restoration, and enables exact computation via correlation matrices and asymptotic description via quasiparticle picture.
AI.motivation: Standard entanglement asymmetry introduces non-Gaussian features in free-fermionic systems, creating analytical limitations. A Gaussian-preserving asymmetry measure is needed to overcome these constraints.
AI.method: The authors define an asymmetry measure that remains strictly within the Gaussian manifold, computed exactly using correlation matrix techniques and described asymptotically through the quasiparticle picture.
AI.result: The proposed measure quantifies the minimal distance between a Gaussian state and the manifold of symmetric Gaussian states, and captures established dynamical signatures such as the Mpemba effect and symmetry restoration.
AI.conclusion: Gaussian asymmetry measures can be computed exactly and efficiently, providing a framework that avoids non-Gaussian complications while preserving the essential physics of entanglement asymmetry.

title: QAOA Parameter Transfer for Hypergraphs
summary: Variational Quantum Algorithms, including the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA), have shown promise in solving optimization problems but rely on costly variational loops that can themselves be hard optimization problems. Many methods have been proposed to mitigate this variational cost, with one of the most common being parameter transfer and concentration where variational parameters for one problem instance or for an average over problem instances can be used as a good set of parameters for another instance. Methods exist for reweighting these parameters based off graph degree and edge weights, but there has been little work on how to do this reweighting to handle higher locality problems where the graph structure turns into a hypergraph structure. In this paper, we analytically derive parameter reweighting rules to transfer parameters between different locality hypergraphs, resulting in a reweighting for the mixing terms in the Hamiltonian which have previously not been considered. These analytics rely on three cycle-free and low-circuit-depth assumptions, but numerics indicate that the results can be used even when these assumptions are not satisfied. The numerics obtain high quality results across a diverse set of hypergraphs with locality less than or equal to five, improving on previous relations that do not reweight the mixing terms.

AI.tldr: This paper derives analytical parameter reweighting rules for transferring variational parameters between hypergraphs of different locality in Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA), focusing on reweighting the mixing terms (previously unaddressed) under three simplifying assumptions, with numerical validation showing improvements across diverse hypergraphs with locality ≤5.
AI.motivation: Variational quantum algorithms like QAOA suffer from costly variational loops. Parameter transfer/concentration can mitigate this cost, but existing reweighting methods (based on graph degree/edge weights) do not handle higher-locality problems where graphs become hypergraphs, and mixing term reweighting has been overlooked.
AI.method: Analytically derive parameter reweighting rules for transferring QAOA parameters between hypergraphs of different locality, specifically reweighting mixing Hamiltonian terms, based on three assumptions (cycle-free and low-circuit-depth). Numerical experiments validate the approach when assumptions are violated.
AI.result: High-quality results across diverse hypergraphs with locality ≤5, outperforming previous methods that do not reweight mixing terms.
AI.conclusion: The derived reweighting rules effectively enable parameter transfer for higher-locality problems, even when theoretical assumptions are not strictly met, improving upon prior work.

title: An Exponential Advantage for Adaptive Tomography of Structured States under Pauli Basis Measurements
summary: Broad claims about whether adaptivity helps in quantum state tomography can be misleading unless the state family, measurement architecture, and error metric are specified carefully. We study a restricted but physically important regime: single-copy quantum state tomography under local Pauli basis measurements, where the allowed measurement settings are tensor-product measurement operators built from local single-qubit Pauli operators, and performance is measured in trace distance with high probability in a minimax sense over a known structured family. We construct an explicit discrete prefix/tree family of states for which adaptive measurement selection achieves polynomial copy complexity, while every non-adaptive design requires exponentially many copies in the worst case. The adaptive upper bound comes from stagewise prefix recovery using hierarchical breadcrumb information revealed by partial prefix matches. The non-adaptive lower bound is based on a rare-prefix mechanism: every fixed design under-samples some deep prefix subset, and outside that subset the competing hypotheses induce identical one-shot laws, so only an exponentially small fraction of the measurement budget contributes to the KL divergence between the full data distributions. The result isolates a concrete regime in which adaptivity provably changes the sample-complexity scaling under the experimentally common local Pauli measurement architecture.

AI.tldr: Under local Pauli basis measurements, adaptive quantum state tomography achieves polynomial copy complexity for a structured state family, while any non-adaptive design requires exponentially many copies in the worst case.
AI.motivation: To resolve whether adaptivity helps in quantum state tomography by specifying a concrete regime (local Pauli measurements, trace distance, minimax over structured families) where the answer is analytically clear.
AI.method: Adaptive upper bound via stagewise prefix recovery using hierarchical breadcrumb information; non-adaptive lower bound via a rare-prefix mechanism where fixed designs under-sample deep prefixes, causing exponentially small KL divergence contribution.
AI.result: Adaptive measurement selection yields polynomial copy complexity, whereas every non-adaptive design requires exponentially many copies in the worst case under the same conditions.
AI.conclusion: There exists a concrete, physically relevant regime under local Pauli measurements where adaptivity provably changes the sample-complexity scaling from exponential to polynomial.

title: Iterative warm-start optimization with quantum imaginary time evolution
summary: Approximate combinatorial optimization is a promising use case for quantum computers. The quantum optimization algorithms often employ a fixed ansatz that evolves an unbiased initial state towards states with better values of the optimand, then samples the states to determine an approximately optimal solution. However, promising alternative approaches have considered ``warm-start" and sampling-based methods that instead begin from the best known solution, which can be directly optimized with the quantum computer and updated as new information becomes available, potentially outperforming the fixed ansätze. Here we use these ideas to design a nonvariational quantum algorithm for combinatorial optimization. At each step the algorithm begins with a state superposed around the best known solution, then drives it to lower energy using quantum imaginary time evolution. These nonvariational, initial-state-dependent circuits are determined using analytic equations that are evaluated using only a conventional computer. After implementing the circuits, the state is sampled, potentially obtaining a new best-known solution to use as the initial state at the next iteration. Using simulations of the algorithm solving MaxCut on 3-regular graphs with 30 or fewer vertices and a shot budget of 100 total shots, the approach obtains median solutions within 95\% of the global optimum and finds optimal solutions in 11\% or more of cases, significantly outperforming random and simplified classical search procedures. We discuss several future directions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a nonvariational quantum algorithm for combinatorial optimization that uses quantum imaginary time evolution starting from a state superposed around the best known solution, updating iteratively as new solutions are sampled. Simulations on MaxCut for 3-regular graphs (≤30 vertices) with 100 shots achieve median solutions within 95% of global optimum and find optimal solutions in ≥11% of cases, outperforming random and simplified classical search.
AI.motivation: To explore alternatives to fixed-ansatz variational quantum optimization by designing an initial-state-dependent, nonvariational algorithm that leverages warm-start and sampling-based methods, potentially outperforming standard fixed ansätze.
AI.method: The algorithm begins each step with a quantum state superposed around the best known solution, then applies quantum imaginary time evolution (nonvariational) to drive the state toward lower energy. The circuits are determined analytically using only classical computation. After circuit implementation, the state is sampled; if a better solution is found, it becomes the initial state for the next iteration.
AI.result: On MaxCut for 3-regular graphs with ≤30 vertices and a budget of 100 total shots, the algorithm achieves median solutions within 95% of the global optimum and finds optimal solutions in ≥11% of cases, significantly outperforming random and simplified classical search procedures.
AI.conclusion: The nonvariational, warm-start quantum algorithm shows promise for combinatorial optimization, outperforming simple classical baselines. The paper discusses future directions for improvement and broader applicability.

title: Solving a Linear System of Equations on a Quantum Computer by Measurement
summary: We present a variational algorithm for fault tolerant quantum computing to solve a system of linear equations which directly maximises the parameters of the target fidelity. This so-called measurement test algorithm can be applied to any computational task with a solution that is represented as eigenvector of a self-adjoint matrix. The solution is prepared as state of a register in the quantum computer by a von Neumann measurement of a corresponding observable, which is implemented using the phase estimation algorithm. The probability to project the system thus into the unknown target state, which equals the target fidelity, is measured in terms of relative frequencies and iteratively optimised to read out the target state. The new algorithm overcomes three issues of previous variational quantum algorithms: i) It does not rely on a decomposition in terms of Pauli strings and therefore can compute eigenvectors of dense matrices. ii) The accuracy is not limited by the condition number $κ$ of the matrix, provided a logarithmic number ($O(\logκ)$) of qubits is used to encode the eigenvalues and iii) the target fidelity $F_T = 1-ε$ can be reached with an accuracy $ε$ that scales with $1/N$ for $N$ measurements per iteration. We demonstrate this by numerical simulations for dense random real-valued $16\times 16$ matrices with non-vanishing determinant.

AI.tldr: A variational quantum algorithm for solving linear systems via von Neumann measurement and phase estimation is presented, overcoming limitations of previous variational approaches by handling dense matrices, avoiding condition number scaling, and achieving 1/N accuracy scaling.
AI.motivation: To overcome three key limitations of existing variational quantum algorithms: reliance on Pauli string decomposition (dense matrices inaccessible), accuracy bounded by condition number κ, and poor fidelity scaling.
AI.method: A measurement test algorithm that directly maximizes target fidelity parameters. The solution is prepared as a quantum register state via von Neumann measurement of an observable, implemented using phase estimation. Target fidelity is measured via relative frequencies and iteratively optimized to read out the target state.
AI.result: The algorithm: i) handles dense matrices without Pauli decomposition, ii) achieves accuracy independent of condition number κ (requires only O(log κ) qubits), iii) reaches target fidelity F_T = 1-ε with accuracy ε scaling as 1/N for N measurements per iteration. Demonstrated via numerical simulations on dense random 16×16 real matrices.
AI.conclusion: The proposed variational algorithm significantly advances fault-tolerant quantum linear system solving by removing previous constraints on matrix density, condition number, and measurement scaling, validated numerically.

title: Mathematical Foundation for Quantum Computing of Electromagnetic Wave Propagation in Dielectric Media
comment: This chapter is part of the book: Emerging Applications of Ions and Plasmas (Editors: Samar K. Guharay & Motoi Wada)
summary: Can quantum computers effectively simulate the propagation and scattering of electromagnetic waves in a classical plasma? This chapter introduces some of the basic concepts in mathematics and physics essential to answering that question. The numerical simulations of Maxwell equations for wave propagation in dielectrics are constrained by technological limitations of the present-day computers. In contrast, there has been ample fanfare around quantum computers and their potential to far exceed the performance of traditional computers. Whether the enhanced capabilities of a quantum computer can be put to use for simulating topics in classical physics is a source of intrigue and curiosity.

AI.tldr: This chapter explores whether quantum computers can simulate electromagnetic wave propagation and scattering in classical plasma, introducing foundational math/physics concepts while contrasting current classical computational limitations with quantum computing's potential.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether quantum computers can effectively simulate classical electromagnetic wave phenomena in plasma, motivated by the curiosity of applying quantum computing's enhanced capabilities to classical physics problems.
AI.method: Introduction of basic mathematical and physics concepts related to quantum computing and electromagnetic wave simulation in plasma.
AI.result: No empirical results presented; the chapter sets up foundational concepts for investigating the research question.
AI.conclusion: The question remains open-ended; quantum computing's potential for classical EM simulation in plasma is described as a source of intrigue requiring further investigation.

title: A Comprehensive Analysis of Accuracy and Robustness in Quantum Neural Networks
summary: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) has recently emerged as a highly promising research frontier. Within this domain, Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs),characterized by Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) at their core and featuring layers of quantum gates optimized by classical algorithms, have garnered significant attention. However, a rigorous and exhaustive evaluation of their practical performance remains largely incomplete. In this study, we conduct a comprehensive comparative analysis of three prominent hybrid classical-quantum architectures: Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN), Quantum Recurrent Neural Networks (QRNN), and Quantum Vision Transformers (QViT), focusing on the critical dimensions of generalization, accuracy, and robustness. Our findings provide novel insights that address previous evaluative gaps. Notably, while these models exhibit exceptional performance on low-feature datasets such as MNIST, their learning efficacy degrades significantly when transitioned to high-feature datasets. Furthermore, convolutional-based models like QCNN appear less effective on high-dimensional data than other machine learning architectures. Additionally, while all models are susceptible to adversarial noise, traditional architectures, such as recurrent and convolutional networks, demonstrate superior resilience. Conversely, in the presence of quantum noise, the transformer-based architecture proves its strength by maintaining high robustness against measurement noise, channel noise, and finite-shot effects, whereas other architectures suffer marked performance declines. These results provide a granular perspective on the current state of the field and underscore the critical importance of tailoring model selection to the constraints of contemporary Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) environments.

AI.tldr: This paper compares three hybrid quantum-classical architectures (QCNN, QRNN, QViT) on generalization, accuracy, and robustness, finding they perform well on low-feature datasets but degrade on high-feature ones; QCNN struggles with high-dimensional data; traditional architectures are more resilient to adversarial noise, while QViT is more robust to quantum noise.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of rigorous and exhaustive evaluation of practical performance in Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) by providing a comprehensive comparative analysis across critical dimensions.
AI.method: Comprehensive comparative analysis of three hybrid classical-quantum architectures (QCNN, QRNN, QViT) evaluating generalization, accuracy, and robustness against both adversarial and quantum noise.
AI.result: All models perform well on low-feature datasets but degrade on high-feature datasets; QCNN is less effective on high-dimensional data; traditional architectures are more resilient to adversarial noise; QViT is more robust to quantum noise (measurement noise, channel noise, finite-shot effects).
AI.conclusion: Model selection must be tailored to NISQ constraints; transformer-based architectures are preferable for quantum-noise-dominated environments, while traditional architectures are better for adversarial-noise-prone settings.

title: One knob to tune them all: Phase-controlled photon statistics and linewidth in partially pumped atomic ensembles
summary: We study a minimal model of collective light emission from an incoherently driven ensemble of atoms where incoherent drive is applied only to a part of atoms and show that both the linewidth and the photon statistics can be controlled within a single framework. In this setting, collective dissipation induces correlations between the pumped and unpumped parts of the system, leading to interference between their emission contributions. By introducing a relative phase between these contributions and tuning the pump rate, we demonstrate that the properties of the emitted light can be varied over a broad range. In particular, the linewidth can be made either independent of system size or scale extensively with it, while the photon statistics can be tuned from antibunched or quantum to bunched. We further show that the role of the relative phase in controlling the interference can alternatively be played by the coherent interaction. By tuning the interaction strength together with the pump rate, one can access the same regimes as in the dissipation-only model. In addition, coherent interactions stabilize regimes of coherent emission with narrow linewidth, reminiscent of superradiant lasing. Our results illustrate how interference in partially driven collective systems provides a flexible mechanism for tailoring both spectral and statistical properties of light.

AI.tldr: A partially incoherently driven atomic ensemble shows that collective dissipation creates correlations between pumped and unpumped atoms, enabling control of emission linewidth (size-independent or extensive) and photon statistics (antibunched to bunched) via relative phase and pump rate, while coherent interactions can replace phase control and stabilize narrow-linewidth superradiant lasing.
AI.motivation: To investigate how collective light emission properties (linewidth and photon statistics) can be controlled within a single minimal framework using a partially driven atomic ensemble, exploring the role of interference between pumped and unpumped subsystems.
AI.method: A minimal model of an incoherently driven atomic ensemble where only a subset of atoms is pumped; collective dissipation induces correlations between pumped and unpumped parts; control via relative phase between emission contributions, pump rate, and coherent interaction strength.
AI.result: Emission linewidth can be made either system-size independent or scale extensively with size; photon statistics tunable from antibunched (quantum) to bunched; coherent interactions can replace the relative phase as a control knob and stabilize narrow-linewidth coherent emission reminiscent of superradiant lasing.
AI.conclusion: Interference in partially driven collective systems provides a flexible mechanism for tailoring both spectral and statistical properties of light, offering a unified control framework.

title: Minimal action shortcut to adiabaticity in a driven Kitaev chain: competing gaps in a topological transition at finite-time
summary: One of the main difficulties in preparing many-body ground states is achieving the target state through simple counterdiabatic controls. For critical systems crossing a transition to a topological phase, this task becomes even more challenging due to the closing of the gaps in multiple symmetry sectors. This is the case of the Kitaev chain, whose transition between the trivial and topological phases involves states belonging to different symmetry sectors. In this work, we apply the recently introduced minimal action shortcut to adiabaticity (MA-STA) to a Kitaev chain and propose a multi-step strategy to obtain the optimal control protocol to drive the system across its different phases. Our results show that high fidelities can be achieved through the adapted MA-STA at time scales much shorter than those of linear ramp protocols. We also compare the performance of both controls in suppressing work fluctuations. These findings may guide the design of STA protocols in many-body systems where competing energy scales and symmetries shape the global dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper applies minimal action shortcut to adiabaticity (MA-STA) to drive a Kitaev chain across topological phase transitions, achieving high fidelities at much shorter timescales than linear ramp protocols, and suppressing work fluctuations.
AI.motivation: Preparing many-body ground states via counterdiabatic controls is difficult, especially for critical systems crossing a topological phase transition where gaps close in multiple symmetry sectors, as in the Kitaev chain.
AI.method: The authors apply minimal action shortcut to adiabaticity (MA-STA) to a Kitaev chain and propose a multi-step strategy to derive the optimal control protocol for driving the system across trivial and topological phases.
AI.result: High fidelities are achieved with MA-STA at time scales much shorter than linear ramp protocols; MA-STA also outperforms linear ramps in suppressing work fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: The findings may guide the design of STA protocols in many-body systems where competing energy scales and symmetries shape the global dynamics.

title: Hardware-Efficient Quantum Optimization for Transportation Networks via Compressed Adiabatic Evolution
summary: Transportation systems such as urban logistics, vehicle routing, and infrastructure planning require solving large-scale combinatorial optimization problems under complex constraints. Problems such as the vehicle routing problem (VRP), traveling salesman problem (TSP), and facility location problem (FLP) involve large discrete search spaces and the need to generate multiple feasible solutions in real time. In this work, we develop a hardware-grounded hybrid quantum optimization framework that uses Approximate Quantum Compilation (AQC) to compress early segments of digitized adiabatic evolution into shallow circuits. The compressed prefix is combined with variational layers, enabling a systematic study of how initialization, circuit depth, and expressivity interact on near-term quantum hardware. All experiments are performed on an IBM gate-based quantum computer, and circuits are evaluated as stochastic generators of candidate transportation plans. Results show that moderate prefix compression reduces two-qubit gate depth while maintaining or improving feasible solution discovery, particularly for routing problems. These benefits depend on compatibility between the compressed prefix and the variational ansatz: while standard QAOA effectively leverages AQC initialization, linear-chain QAOA shows limited improvement. Overall, this work demonstrates that hybrid AQC-QAOA methods provide a practical pathway for hardware-efficient quantum optimization, positioning quantum algorithms as candidate generators within transportation decision-making workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid quantum optimization framework combining Approximate Quantum Compilation (AQC) with variational layers to compress adiabatic evolution into shallow circuits for solving large-scale transportation combinatorial optimization problems (VRP, TSP, FLP). Experiments on IBM gate-based quantum hardware show moderate prefix compression reduces two-qubit gate depth while maintaining or improving feasible solution discovery, especially for routing problems, though benefits depend on compatibility with the variational ansatz.
AI.motivation: Transportation systems (urban logistics, vehicle routing, infrastructure planning) require solving large-scale combinatorial optimization problems under complex constraints, needing real-time generation of multiple feasible solutions. Current quantum methods face hardware limitations that hinder practical deployment.
AI.method: A hardware-grounded hybrid quantum optimization framework using Approximate Quantum Compilation (AQC) to compress early segments of digitized adiabatic evolution into shallow circuits, combined with variational layers. Systematic study of initialization, circuit depth, and expressivity on IBM gate-based quantum hardware, with circuits evaluated as stochastic generators of candidate transportation plans.
AI.result: Moderate prefix compression reduces two-qubit gate depth while maintaining or improving feasible solution discovery, particularly for routing problems. Standard QAOA effectively leverages AQC initialization, but linear-chain QAOA shows limited improvement.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid AQC-QAOA methods provide a practical pathway for hardware-efficient quantum optimization, positioning quantum algorithms as candidate generators within transportation decision-making workflows. Benefits depend on compatibility between the compressed prefix and the variational ansatz.

title: Qvine: Vine Structured Quantum Circuits for Loading High Dimensional Distributions
summary:
Loading high dimensional distributions is an important task for utilizing quantum computers on applications ranging from machine learning to finance.
