The high dimensionality leads to a curse of dimensionality, representing a d-dimensional distribution with k resolution requires dk qubits and an unstructured parameterized circuit would express a unitary in an exponential operator space in the number of qubits, leading to vanishing gradients and poor convergence guarantees even at high depth.
  Vine copula decompositions are widely used to represent high dimensional distributions classically, showing high quality approximation in many important applications, such as financial modeling. We present Qvine, a vine structured ansatz for quantum circuits, that mirrors the vine decomposition to construct scalable quantum circuits with efficient trainability while achieving similarly high quality approximation for amplitude encoding distributions. For regular vines (R-vines), we show that the circuit depth scales at most quadratic in the dimension of the distribution, while for D-vines, as well as many practical R-vines, the circuit depth scales linear in the dimension. For 3-dimensional and 4-dimensional Gaussians and empirical joint stock price return distributions for selected stocks, our experiments show Qvines achieve high quality loading.

AI.tldr: Qvine introduces a vine copula-inspired quantum circuit ansatz for efficiently loading high-dimensional distributions, with circuit depth scaling linearly to quadratically in dimension, avoiding the curse of dimensionality and vanishing gradients.
AI.motivation: Loading high-dimensional distributions on quantum computers suffers from the curse of dimensionality—requiring exponentially many parameters leads to vanishing gradients and poor convergence. Classical vine copula decompositions handle high-dimensional distributions effectively, motivating a quantum analogue.
AI.method: Qvine mirrors vine copula decomposition to construct scalable quantum circuit ansätze. For regular vines (R-vines), circuit depth scales at most quadratically in dimension; for D-vines and many practical R-vines, depth scales linearly. The ansatz is designed for amplitude encoding of distributions.
AI.result: Experiments on 3D and 4D Gaussian distributions and empirical joint stock price return distributions demonstrate that Qvine achieves high-quality loading with efficient trainability.
AI.conclusion: Qvine provides a classically-inspired, trainable quantum circuit ansatz that overcomes the curse of dimensionality for loading high-dimensional distributions, with practical applications in finance and machine learning.

title: qSHIFT: An Adaptive Sampling Protocol for Higher-Order Quantum Simulation
summary: Quantum simulation is a cornerstone application for quantum computing, yet standard methods face a trade-off between circuit depth and accuracy: Trotterization depth scales with the number of Hamiltonian terms $L$, while sampling-based qDRIFT is restricted to $O(t^2)$ error scaling. Here, We introduce qSHIFT, an adaptive sampling protocol that overcomes these limitations. By adaptively updating sampling distributions, qSHIFT maintains $L$-independent gate complexity while achieving an improved error scaling of $O(t^{1+r})$ for an adjustable parameter $r$. This performance is enabled by a classical subroutine solving $L^r$ linear equations per sampling round. Numerical demonstrations confirm the $O(t^{1+r})$ scaling, showcasing qSHIFT as a resource-efficient framework for high-precision quantum simulation. Furthermore, the protocol's reduced circuit depth enhances its compatibility with physical error mitigation, making it a promising candidate for implementation on near-term quantum devices. In addition to its role as a standalone algorithm, qSHIFT can provide a high-precision foundation for modular quantum frameworks such as qSWIFT or Krylov quantum diagonalization.

AI.tldr: qSHIFT is an adaptive sampling protocol for quantum simulation that achieves L-independent gate complexity and improved error scaling of O(t^{1+r}) by solving L^r linear equations per sampling round, overcoming the trade-off between circuit depth and accuracy in standard methods like Trotterization and qDRIFT.
AI.motivation: Standard quantum simulation methods face a trade-off between circuit depth and accuracy: Trotterization depth scales with the number of Hamiltonian terms L, while qDRIFT is restricted to O(t^2) error scaling. The goal is to overcome these limitations simultaneously.
AI.method: qSHIFT employs an adaptive sampling protocol that dynamically updates sampling distributions. It incorporates a classical subroutine that solves L^r linear equations per sampling round, enabling an adjustable parameter r that controls the error scaling.
AI.result: qSHIFT achieves L-independent gate complexity and improved error scaling of O(t^{1+r}) for an adjustable parameter r, as confirmed by numerical demonstrations. It also enhances compatibility with physical error mitigation and can serve as a foundation for modular quantum frameworks.
AI.conclusion: qSHIFT is a resource-efficient framework for high-precision quantum simulation that overcomes key limitations of existing methods, making it a promising candidate for near-term quantum devices and modular quantum simulation frameworks.

title: Algebraic quantum kinematics and SR-selection
summary: We develop, as the first of a six-paper series, an operator-algebraic framework relating non-relativistic quantum mechanics and special relativity. Three structural facts organize the framework. (i)~The photon sector of free QED is a transparent realization: classical Fourier--Maxwell theory supplies a complex Hilbert-space scaffold (inner product, symplectic form, Schrödinger-form mode equation, polarization $\mathbb{C}^2$) with no quantum input, and a single canonical commutator with scale $\hbar$ on the mode amplitudes promotes it to single-photon QED, with photon indivisibility, the Planck relation $E=\hbarω$, and the spin spectrum $\pm\hbar$ as theorems. (ii)~The constants $c$ and $\hbar$ play non-interchangeable roles: $c$ is intrinsic to each Fourier-conjugate space, while $\hbar$ acts \emph{between} them, converting kinematic phase rates into dynamical observables. (iii)~Lifting the framework to a Haag--Kastler net with sharper-than-equal-time microcausality and positive-energy spectrum is structurally obstructed in the Galilean case; we state this as the SR-selection conjecture and identify three strands of evidence (Hegerfeldt spreading; absence of a known Galilean multi-particle resolution; Reeh--Schlieder failure on Galilean Haag--Kastler nets), the third established as a precise no-go theorem in the second paper of the series. The framework's modular content (Tomita--Takesaki, Bisognano--Wichmann, Unruh as KMS, type-$\mathrm{III}_1$ universality) is collected as the algebraic substrate on which the curved-background, dynamical-metric, and crossed-product extensions of the third through sixth papers operate.

AI.tldr: This first of six papers develops an operator-algebraic framework connecting non-relativistic quantum mechanics and special relativity, showing: (i) classical Fourier-Maxwell theory provides the scaffold for single-photon QED with a single commutator; (ii) c and ℏ play distinct, non-interchangeable roles; (iii) lifting the framework to a Haag-Kastler net faces structural obstruction in the Galilean case (SR-selection conjecture), with three strands of evidence including a no-go theorem in Paper II.
AI.motivation: To construct a rigorous operator-algebraic framework that bridges non-relativistic quantum mechanics and special relativity, clarifying the distinct roles of c and ℏ, and to identify why Galilean relativity fails to support a consistent Haag-Kastler net with microcausality and positive-energy spectrum.
AI.method: The authors build the framework from three structural facts: (i) photon sector of free QED as a transparent realization starting from classical Fourier-Maxwell theory; (ii) non-interchangeable roles of c (intrinsic to Fourier-conjugate spaces) and ℏ (acting between them); (iii) analysis of the obstruction to lifting the framework to a Haag-Kastler net in the Galilean case, supported by evidence from Hegerfeldt spreading, absence of Galilean multi-particle resolution, and Reeh-Schlieder failure (formalized as a no-go theorem in Paper II).
AI.result: Photon indivisibility, the Planck relation E=ℏω, and spin spectrum ±ℏ emerge as theorems from classical Maxwell theory plus a single commutator. The SR-selection conjecture is articulated with three strands of evidence, one of which is a precise no-go theorem deferred to Paper II. The modular content (Tomita-Takesaki, Bisognano-Wichmann, Unruh as KMS, type-III₁ universality) is collected as the algebraic substrate for subsequent curved-background and dynamical-metric extensions.
AI.conclusion: The framework establishes a foundational operator-algebraic bridge between non-relativistic QM and special relativity, with clearly separated roles for c and ℏ, and demonstrates a structural obstruction to Galilean relativistic Haag-Kastler nets, setting the stage for curved-background and dynamical-metric extensions in Papers III-VI.

title: Galilean Reeh--Schlieder Obstruction
summary: We prove that the standard Galilean Haag--Kastler axioms, augmented by Bargmann mass superselection, are inconsistent with the Reeh--Schlieder property: no such net admits a vacuum that is cyclic and separating for every local field algebra. Two ingredients combine: Galilean Schrödinger fields annihilate the Fock vacuum, and Bargmann mass superselection forbids the Hermitian-combination evasion that keeps relativistic axioms consistent. The result extends beyond the Fock-representation hypothesis: any Galilean Haag--Kastler net whose canonical fields carry definite Bargmann mass charges and admit time-zero restrictions on a field-algebra-stable common dense domain is incompatible with Reeh--Schlieder. The bounded-below mass spectrum and the vacuum-at-spectral-minimum, usually imposed as separate axioms, are derived consequences -- of positive-energy boost positivity and a Bose-CCR algebraic descent, respectively. The Tomita--Takesaki modular flow is consequently unavailable on Galilean local field algebras. We identify the Reeh--Schlieder property as the precise structural ingredient distinguishing relativistic from Galilean algebraic quantum field theory: relativistic AQFT has it as a theorem, Galilean AQFT cannot.

AI.tldr: The Galilean Haag-Kastler axioms with Bargmann mass superselection are incompatible with the Reeh-Schlieder property, which distinguishes relativistic from Galilean algebraic quantum field theory.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that the Reeh-Schlieder property, a standard theorem in relativistic AQFT, cannot hold in Galilean-invariant algebraic quantum field theories due to the combination of Schrödinger fields annihilating the Fock vacuum and Bargmann mass superselection blocking relativistic evasion strategies.
AI.method: Formal axiomatic analysis within the Haag-Kastler framework: augmenting Galilean axioms with Bargmann mass superselection, examining time-zero field restrictions on a common dense domain, and deriving bounded-below mass spectrum and vacuum-at-spectral-minimum from positive-energy boost positivity and Bose-CCR algebraic descent.
AI.result: No Galilean Haag-Kastler net satisfying the stated conditions admits a vacuum that is cyclic and separating for every local field algebra; Tomita-Takesaki modular flow is unavailable on Galilean local field algebras; the Reeh-Schlieder property is identified as the precise structural distinction between relativistic and Galilean AQFT.
AI.conclusion: The Reeh-Schlieder property is a theorem in relativistic AQFT but cannot hold in Galilean AQFT, establishing a fundamental structural incompatibility between Galilean symmetry and this key algebraic property.

title: Quantum Grover Adaptive Search for Discrete Simulation Optimization
summary: Quantum computing has advanced rapidly in recent years and has shown advantages in a variety of domains. In this paper, we investigate its potential for discrete simulation optimization in the fixed-confidence setting, a fundamental problem in the simulation literature. We first introduce a quantum simulation oracle that prepares a coherent superposition over all candidate solutions and provides the foundation for quantum algorithm design. Based on this oracle, we develop the first Grover-search-based quantum algorithm for discrete simulation optimization, called SOGAS. In particular, SOGAS uses a binary-search framework to progressively eliminate suboptimal solutions while carefully controlling the error probability, and eventually identifies a set of near-optimal solutions. We prove that SOGAS returns a near-optimal solution with probability at least the prescribed confidence level and achieves a quadratic speedup in the dependence of query complexity on the number of candidate solutions. Numerical experiments further show that SOGAS substantially outperforms classical benchmarks and provide empirical evidence for quantum advantage in discrete simulation optimization.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SOGAS, the first Grover-search-based quantum algorithm for discrete simulation optimization in the fixed-confidence setting. It achieves quadratic speedup in query complexity relative to the number of candidate solutions and demonstrates significant empirical improvement over classical benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To investigate the potential of quantum computing for discrete simulation optimization in the fixed-confidence setting, a fundamental problem where quantum speedup has not been previously explored.
AI.method: Introduces a quantum simulation oracle that prepares a coherent superposition over all candidate solutions, then develops SOGAS—a Grover-search-based algorithm that uses a binary-search framework to progressively eliminate suboptimal solutions while controlling error probability.
AI.result: SOGAS returns a near-optimal solution with probability at least the prescribed confidence level, achieves quadratic speedup in query complexity dependence on the number of candidate solutions, and substantially outperforms classical benchmarks in numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quantum algorithm provides empirical evidence for quantum advantage in discrete simulation optimization.

title: Newton-Cartan limit of Klein-Gordon AQFT and the collapse of Galilean modular structure
summary: We extend the established Galilean/relativistic structural divider in algebraic quantum field theory, namely, the absence of Reeh-Schlieder and of Tomita-Takesaki modular flow on local algebras of any Galilean Haag-Kastler net satisfying a natural axiom set augmented by the Bargmann-charge hypotheses (G7$^*$)(a) and (G7$^*$)(d) to curved backgrounds via the Newton-Cartan ($c\to\infty$) limit. We show, for the free Klein-Gordon field on Minkowski and on static globally hyperbolic spacetimes admitting a Post-Newtonian expansion, that a position-independent rest-energy rescaling produces in the limit a Galilean Haag-Kastler net satisfying the axioms of Ref. [1] in flat-space form (Minkowski) or in a curved-space modification (Killing-flow invariance and uniqueness of the vacuum replacing full translation invariance) appropriate to the static case. The Bargmann central charge equals the Klein--Gordon mass~$m$; the gravitational potential $V(x)$ enters the limiting Schrödinger Hamiltonian but not the algebraic structure obstructed by the Galilean Reeh-Schlieder no-go theorem. The strengthened obstruction theorem of Ref. [1] extends to the modified curved-space setting on Fock representations, and the limiting net carries no modular flow on local algebras. Schwarzschild is treated as a worked example: the Killing horizon shrinks to a point, the Hartle-Hawking thermal state has no $c\to\infty$ limit, and the Boulware vacuum limits to the gravitational hydrogenic ground state. The Reissner-Nordström metric is included as a sanity check confirming that leading Post-Newtonian misses the electromagnetic content of the background. We discuss how Newton's constant~$G$ enters the present (background-metric) framework only at the level of the limiting Hamiltonian, and indicate where dynamical-metric extensions would require $G$ to play a structural role.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the Galilean/relativistic structural divide in algebraic QFT to curved backgrounds via the Newton-Cartan limit, showing that the Galilean Reeh-Schlieder obstruction and absence of modular flow persist on static globally hyperbolic spacetimes with Post-Newtonian expansion, with Schwarzschild and Reissner-Nordström as examples.
AI.motivation: To extend the previously established Galilean obstruction theorems (absence of Reeh-Schlieder property and Tomita-Takesaki modular flow) from flat spacetime to curved backgrounds, bridging algebraic QFT and Newton-Cartan geometry.
AI.method: Taking the c→∞ (Newton-Cartan) limit of the free Klein-Gordon field on Minkowski and static globally hyperbolic spacetimes with Post-Newtonian expansion, with a position-independent rest-energy rescaling, producing a Galilean Haag-Kastler net satisfying modified axioms (Killing-flow invariance, unique vacuum).
AI.result: The Bargmann central charge equals the Klein-Gordon mass m; the gravitational potential appears only in the limiting Schrödinger Hamiltonian, not in the algebraic structure; the strengthened obstruction theorem extends to curved Fock representations; the limiting net has no modular flow on local algebras; Schwarzschild's Killing horizon shrinks to a point; Hartle-Hawking state has no c→∞ limit; Boulware vacuum limits to gravitational hydrogenic ground state.
AI.conclusion: The Galilean obstruction to Reeh-Schlieder and modular flow is robust in curved backgrounds with Post-Newtonian expansion; Newton's constant enters only at the Hamiltonian level in the background-metric framework, while dynamical-metric extensions would require a structural role for G.

title: Recent Advances in Quantum Architecture Search
summary: Variational quantum algorithms (VQAs) constitute a prominent framework for exploring the capabilities of near-term quantum computers. As the effectiveness of VQAs depends heavily on the design of variational quantum circuits, Quantum Architecture Search (QAS) has emerged as a critical research area to automate the discovery of high-performing circuit structures. This paper reviews key advancements in current research on QAS, including its core concepts, representative methodologies, and applications. The content is structured to ensure broad accessibility for a diverse audience of researchers while preserving the core principles of complex methodologies. In addition, we discuss remaining challenges and suggest potential research directions to offer perspectives on future exploration in this rapidly evolving field.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews advancements in Quantum Architecture Search (QAS), a method to automate the design of variational quantum circuits for near-term quantum computers, covering core concepts, methodologies, applications, challenges, and future directions.
AI.motivation: The effectiveness of Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) heavily depends on the design of variational quantum circuits, motivating the need for automated discovery of high-performing circuit structures through Quantum Architecture Search (QAS).
AI.method: The paper presents a structured review of QAS, including core concepts, representative methodologies, and applications, designed for broad accessibility while maintaining the core principles of complex methodologies.
AI.result: A comprehensive overview of key advancements in QAS research, covering core concepts, representative methodologies, and applications.
AI.conclusion: The paper discusses remaining challenges and suggests potential research directions for future exploration in the rapidly evolving field of QAS.

title: Amplitude Encoding of Slater-Type Orbitals via Matrix Product States: Efficient State Preparation and Integral Evaluation on Quantum Hardware
summary: Slater-type orbitals (STOs) provide the physically correct description of atomic wavefunctions but have been largely replaced by Gaussian-type orbitals in computational chemistry due to the lack of closed-form multi-center integrals. We present a systematic study of amplitude encoding of STOs on quantum computers using matrix product states (MPS). For one-dimensional orbital functions of the form $p_d(x) e^{-ζx}$, we derive analytical MPS constructions with constant bond dimension $χ= d + 1$, requiring $O(n)$ classical and quantum resources for $n$-qubit registers with no grid sampling. We demonstrate a complete one-electron integral pipeline -- overlap, kinetic energy, and nuclear attraction -- in one dimension, validating the overlap and kinetic energy on IBM Heron processors at 5~qubits with 0.67\% hardware-induced error using Zero-Noise Extrapolation. In three dimensions, we compute multi-center overlap integrals between 1s and 2s orbitals in Cartesian coordinates with 0.02\% discretization error at 18~qubits. A systematic entanglement analysis reveals that the MPS bond dimension of three-dimensional STOs in Cartesian coordinates saturates with increasing grid resolution -- reaching $\sim$138 for the hydrogen 1s orbital at 12~qubits per coordinate -- establishing bounded encoding complexity rather than the exponential scaling initially expected. The SVD truncation threshold provides a practical resource parameter, reducing the bond dimension to 39 at threshold $10^{-6}$ with negligible accuracy loss. These results map the entanglement landscape for amplitude encoding of atomic orbitals and establish MPS-based state preparation as a viable path toward exact STO basis sets on quantum computers.

AI.tldr: This paper presents amplitude encoding of Slater-type orbitals (STOs) on quantum computers using matrix product states (MPS). For 1D STOs, analytical MPS constructions achieve constant bond dimension χ = d+1 with O(n) resources. They validate a one-electron integral pipeline (overlap, kinetic energy, nuclear attraction) on IBM Heron processors at 5 qubits with 0.67% error. For 3D, multi-center overlaps between 1s and 2s orbitals achieve 0.02% discretization error at 18 qubits. Entanglement analysis shows MPS bond dimension for 3D Cartesian STOs saturates with grid resolution (~138 for H 1s at 12 qubits/coordinate), establishing bounded encoding complexity.
AI.motivation: STOs provide physically correct atomic wavefunction descriptions but lack closed-form multi-center integrals, leading to their replacement by GTOs in computational chemistry. The goal is to establish whether MPS-based amplitude encoding can make exact STO basis sets viable on quantum computers, overcoming the historical limitation.
AI.method: The authors derive analytical MPS constructions for 1D STOs of form p_d(x)e^{-ζx} with constant bond dimension χ = d+1, requiring O(n) resources. For 3D, they encode STOs as product of per-coordinate MPS representations in Cartesian coordinates. They compute integrals (overlap, kinetic energy, nuclear attraction) via inner products of MPS-encoded states. Error mitigation uses Zero-Noise Extrapolation on hardware.
AI.result: 1D overlap and kinetic energy validated on IBM Heron at 5 qubits with 0.67% hardware error (ZNE). 3D multi-center overlap integrals for 1s/2s orbitals achieve 0.02% discretization error at 18 qubits. MPS bond dimension for 3D STOs saturates with grid resolution (~138 for H 1s at 12 qubits/coordinate), far below exponential scaling. SVD truncation at 10^{-6} reduces bond dimension to 39 with negligible accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: MPS-based amplitude encoding provides bounded complexity for STO encoding on quantum computers, contradicting expectations of exponential scaling. This establishes a viable path toward exact STO basis sets on quantum computers, enabling physically correct atomic orbital representations in quantum computational chemistry.

title: Schroedinger's Equation at 100: The Wave Picture That Helped and Possibly Hurt
summary: Schroedinger's equation gave early quantum theory a visual language that looked like physics again: a wave evolving by a linear differential equation. This essay argues that the same success also seeded a recurring impulse to keep quantum theory "classical-looking" by treating the wave function as a physical wave. Schroedinger quickly realized that, for many-particle systems, the wave function is naturally defined on configuration space rather than ordinary physical space, blocking any straightforward reading of it as a literal classical wave. Read through Mach and Boltzmann, who shaped his intellectual outlook most deeply, his achievement appears double-edged: it provided an extraordinarily powerful picture for calculation and discovery, while also warning against taking that picture too literally. I argue that this tension never fully disappeared. It still reappears in modern physics whenever the wave function, or in quantum field theory the field itself, is treated as ontology rather than as part of a representation tied to measurement and observational context, a point sharpened by Bell-type no-go theorems. The centenary moral is: use pictures boldly, but demote them ontologically.

AI.tldr: Schrödinger's equation gave quantum theory a visual, classical-like language (wave mechanics), but this success created a recurring impulse to treat the wave function as a physical wave—an impulse Schrödinger himself recognized as problematic for many-particle systems defined on configuration space. Influenced by Mach and Boltzmann, his work was double-edged: a powerful calculational tool alongside a warning against literal interpretation. This tension persists in modern debates about wave function/field ontology, sharpened by Bell-type theorems. The lesson: use pictures boldly but treat them ontologically as representations, not reality.
AI.motivation: To examine how Schrödinger's equation, despite its success in giving quantum theory a visualizable "classical-looking" form, inadvertently created a persistent tension between the pragmatic utility of pictorial thinking and the ontological misinterpretation of the wave function as a literal physical wave.
AI.method: Historical-philosophical analysis tracing Schrödinger's intellectual debts to Mach and Boltzmann, contrasting single-particle wave mechanics with the configuration-space nature of many-particle wave functions, and connecting this historical tension to modern quantum foundations (wave function realism, quantum field ontology, Bell-type no-go theorems).
AI.result: Schrödinger's achievement was double-edged: it provided a powerful visual language for calculation and discovery, while simultaneously containing built-in warnings (configuration space) against taking the picture too literally. The tension between pictorial utility and ontological caution has persisted into modern debates, where wave function or field realism runs afoul of measurement contextuality and Bell-type constraints.
AI.conclusion: The centenary lesson is to use pictures boldly as heuristic and calculational tools, but to demote them ontologically—treating them as representations tied to measurement and observational context rather than as literal descriptions of physical reality.

title: System-Level Design of Scalable Fluxonium Quantum Processors with Double-Transmon Couplers
summary: Fluxonium qubits combine long coherence times with strong anharmonicity, making them a promising platform for scalable superconducting quantum processors. Recent experiments have demonstrated high-fidelity operations in multi-qubit processors while suppressing stray qubit interactions using fluxonium-transmon-fluxonium (FTF) architectures. However, scaling such systems to larger arrays is constrained by a trade-off between achievable coupling strength, crosstalk suppression and qubit-qubit spacing required for wiring in a two-dimensional architecture. Multimode couplers, such as the double-transmon coupler (DTC), provide a promising pathway to overcome this limitation by enabling stronger interactions without compromising qubit spacing and isolation. Here, we develop a quantitative design framework for fluxonium-based quantum processors employing DTCs. Central to this work is a frequency-partitioned architecture that places qubit transitions, tunable-coupler excitations, and resonator modes in well-separated spectral regions. This structured allocation reduces parameter interdependence and enables the concurrent optimization of gate operations, readout, and qubit reset. By formulating device design as a multi-objective optimization problem under realistic experimental constraints and fabrication-induced disorder, we develop a tractable sequential workflow and determine a feasible parameter regime that simultaneously supports high-fidelity single- and two-qubit gates, fast qubit reset, and robust dispersive readout. These results establish a system-level architectural methodology that links circuit parameters to processor-level performance, and provide an experimentally actionable pathway toward scalable fluxonium quantum processors.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a quantitative design framework for fluxonium quantum processors using double-transmon couplers (DTCs), employing a frequency-partitioned architecture that separates qubit transitions, coupler excitations, and resonator modes into distinct spectral regions. The authors formulate device design as a multi-objective optimization problem under experimental constraints and fabrication disorder, demonstrating a feasible parameter regime that simultaneously supports high-fidelity single- and two-qubit gates, fast reset, and robust readout.
AI.motivation: Scaling fluxonium-based superconducting quantum processors to larger arrays is constrained by a trade-off between coupling strength, crosstalk suppression, and qubit-qubit spacing required for wiring in 2D architectures. Multimode couplers like the DTC offer a promising solution but lack a systematic design framework.
AI.method: The authors develop a quantitative design framework using a frequency-partitioned architecture with well-separated spectral regions for qubit transitions, tunable-coupler excitations, and resonator modes. They formulate device design as a multi-objective optimization problem under realistic experimental constraints and fabrication-induced disorder, implemented via a tractable sequential workflow.
AI.result: The optimization workflow identifies a feasible parameter regime that concurrently supports high-fidelity single- and two-qubit gates, fast qubit reset, and robust dispersive readout, overcoming the coupling-crosstalk-spacing trade-off inherent in prior FTF architectures.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a system-level architectural methodology linking circuit parameters to processor-level performance, providing an experimentally actionable pathway toward scalable fluxonium quantum processors with DTC-based multimode couplers.

title: Quantum Complexity and New Directions in Nuclear Physics and High-Energy Physics Phenomenology
comment: 64 pages, 45 figures. Invited review article for the European Physical Journal A
summary: Advances in quantum information science (QIS) are providing transformative insights into the complexity of quantum many-body systems, potentially defining new frontiers in nuclear and high-energy physics. This review explores how QIS-derived techniques are fostering new analytic frameworks and algorithms - both classical and quantum - to tackle (some of the) present barriers to discovery in fundamental physics, with applicability to other science domains. We highlight how these techniques are shedding new light on the structure and dynamics of hadrons, nuclei, matter in extreme conditions, and beyond. Importantly, they are expected to play an essential role in the development of large-scale quantum simulations of such systems, particularly in setting the balance among quantum and classical computational resources.

AI.tldr: This review explores how quantum information science (QIS) techniques are providing new analytic frameworks and classical/quantum algorithms to overcome barriers in nuclear and high-energy physics, with applications to hadrons, nuclei, and extreme matter, while also guiding resource allocation for large-scale quantum simulations.
AI.motivation: QIS advances are offering transformative insights into quantum many-body complexity, potentially defining new frontiers and overcoming present barriers to discovery in nuclear and high-energy physics.
AI.method: The paper reviews how QIS-derived techniques foster new analytic frameworks and algorithms (both classical and quantum) to address computational barriers in fundamental physics.
AI.result: These techniques shed new light on the structure and dynamics of hadrons, nuclei, matter in extreme conditions, and are expected to play an essential role in large-scale quantum simulations.
AI.conclusion: QIS techniques will be crucial for balancing quantum and classical computational resources in future large-scale simulations of quantum many-body systems in physics.

title: Genuine tripartite entanglement in Bhabha scattering with an entangled spectator particle
comment: 13 pages, 8 figures, comments are welcomed. Accepted by Physical Review D
summary: From the perspective of quantum information science, we investigate tree-level Bhabha scattering between an incident electron $A$ and a positron B, where $B$ is initially entangled with a spectator electron $C$, which does not participate in the scattering interaction.We find that the quantum electrodynamics (QED) scattering between $A$ and $B$ can drive the global $ABC$ system into a genuine tripartite entangled (GTE) state. Using four canonical tripartite entanglement metrics, we systematically characterize and quantify the GTE of the composite system, and demonstrate that the scattering momentum of the $A$-$B$ pair and the initial $B$-$C$ entanglement are the key resources governing GTE generation.We further analyze the monogamy of quantum correlations, which imposes fundamental constraints on the shareability of quantum resources in multipartite systems. Specifically, we systematically study the monogamy relations for the squared entanglement of formation and squared quantum discord in our scattering model, and find that monogamy constraints are markedly relaxed in the non-relativistic regime, enabling enhanced shareability of quantum correlations across the three particles. This work uncovers novel quantum correlation properties of fundamental QED scattering processes, and provides direct theoretical guidance for the development of QED-based quantum information processing protocols.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates tree-level Bhabha scattering where an incident electron scatters with a positron that is initially entangled with a spectator electron. It shows that the QED scattering process generates genuine tripartite entanglement (GTE) in the global system, characterized via four metrics, and reveals that monogamy constraints on quantum correlations are significantly relaxed in the non-relativistic regime.
AI.motivation: To explore quantum correlation properties of fundamental QED scattering processes from a quantum information perspective, and to understand how relativistic scattering can generate multipartite entanglement and manipulate quantum resource shareability.
AI.method: The authors study tree-level Bhabha scattering with an initially entangled electron-positron pair and a spectator electron. They use four canonical tripartite entanglement metrics to quantify GTE, and analyze monogamy relations using squared entanglement of formation and squared quantum discord.
AI.result: The QED scattering drives the global ABC system into a genuine tripartite entangled state. The scattering momentum and initial B-C entanglement are key resources for GTE generation. Monogamy constraints are markedly relaxed in the non-relativistic regime, allowing enhanced shareability of quantum correlations.
AI.conclusion: This work uncovers novel quantum correlation properties in fundamental QED scattering and provides theoretical guidance for developing QED-based quantum information processing protocols.

title: Imaginarity-generating power of unitaries: A resource-theoretic approach
summary: Imaginarity, stemming from the complex structure of quantum mechanics, has recently emerged as a fundamental resource, yet its dynamical generation remains largely unexplored. In this work, we introduce the notion of imaginarity-generating power (IGP) of unitary dynamics, which quantifies the ability of unitary operations to produce imaginarity from initially real quantum states. To quantify imaginarity, we employ a measure based on the Hilbert--Schmidt norm, which we show to be monotone under real unital operations. Within the framework of dynamical resource theories, we derive an exact expression for the purity-constrained IGP in arbitrary dimensions and show that, for pure real input states, it depends solely on intrinsic and experimentally accessible properties of the unitary. We further analyze its average behavior over ensembles of states with varying purity under both uniform and Hilbert--Schmidt distributions. We prove that it satisfies the essential properties of a valid resource monotone within the dynamical resource theory of imaginarity. We also characterize the unitaries that maximize the IGP and determine the corresponding bounds. Moreover, for Haar-random unitaries, we show that the IGP concentrates near its maximal value in high dimensions with small fluctuations, indicating that typical high-dimensional quantum dynamics are highly effective at generating imaginarity.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the imaginarity-generating power (IGP) of unitary dynamics, quantifying how unitary operations generate imaginarity from real quantum states. Using a Hilbert-Schmidt norm measure, the authors derive exact expressions for IGP under purity constraints, analyze average behavior, prove resource monotone properties, characterize maximally generating unitaries, and show that Haar-random unitaries in high dimensions concentrate near maximal IGP.
AI.motivation: Imaginarity has recently been recognized as a fundamental quantum resource, but its dynamical generation has remained largely unexplored. This work aims to fill that gap by developing a framework to quantify how unitary dynamics produce imaginarity.
AI.method: The authors employ a Hilbert-Schmidt norm-based measure of imaginarity, prove it is monotone under real unital operations, derive exact analytical expressions for purity-constrained IGP, analyze average behavior under uniform and Hilbert-Schmidt distributions, and use concentration of measure techniques for Haar-random unitaries.
AI.result: An exact expression for purity-constrained IGP in arbitrary dimensions is derived, showing it depends on intrinsic and experimentally accessible properties of the unitary for pure real inputs. The IGP satisfies essential properties of a dynamical resource monotone. The maximal IGP and corresponding unitaries are characterized. For Haar-random unitaries, IGP concentrates near its maximum in high dimensions with small fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: Typical high-dimensional quantum dynamics are highly effective at generating imaginarity, as Haar-random unitaries near-maximally generate imaginarity with minimal fluctuations. The framework provides experimentally accessible tools for quantifying imaginarity generation in quantum dynamics.

title: Quantum Gatekeeper: Multi-Factor Context-Bound Image Steganography with VQC Based Key Derivation on Quantum Hardware
summary: This paper presents Quantum Gatekeeper, a context-bound image steganography framework where successful payload recovery depends on both cryptographic decryption and the reconstruction of a precise extraction path. The system integrates lossless least significant bit (LSB) embedding with a deterministic variational quantum circuit (VQC)-derived gate key, multi-factor contextual binding, and authenticated encryption. Payload extraction is contingent upon four requisite factors: a password, a shared secret, a user-supplied context string, and a reference image signature. Any deviation in these factors causes the system to read from an incorrect pixel sequence or fail authentication, resulting in silent rejection rather than partial disclosure. The proposed method derives a gatecontrolled extraction key from a seed-conditioned variational circuit, with parameters generated via cryptographic hash expansion and context-dependent image features. To ensure encode/decode consistency, the cryptographic key path is generated via exact statevector simulation; concurrently, IBM superconducting quantum hardware is utilized to evaluate the statistical behavior of the circuit family under physical noise. We introduce a dual-region image layout to resolve the nonce bootstrapping dependency, separating header recovery from payload recovery through independently derived keys. Experimental results confirm successful end-to-end message embedding and recovery on PNG images, demonstrating deterministic success under correct conditions and failure otherwise. The framework supports both text and image payloads; in the image-in-image configuration, a secret image is resized to a fixed resolution prior to embedding, enabling exact pixel-level recovery under correct contextual reconstruction.

AI.tldr: Quantum Gatekeeper is a context-bound image steganography framework combining LSB embedding with variational quantum circuit-derived keys, requiring four contextual factors (password, shared secret, context string, reference image signature) for payload recovery; it uses exact statevector simulation for deterministic extraction and IBM quantum hardware for noise analysis, achieving exact pixel-level recovery under correct conditions and silent rejection otherwise.
AI.motivation: To develop a steganography system where payload recovery is contingent upon multiple contextual factors, preventing partial disclosure under incorrect conditions and enhancing security beyond traditional key-based approaches.
AI.method: Integrates lossless LSB embedding with a VQC-derived gate key, multi-factor contextual binding (password, shared secret, context string, image signature), authenticated encryption, seed-conditioned variational circuit with cryptographic hash expansion, exact statevector simulation for deterministic key path generation, IBM quantum hardware for noise evaluation, and a dual-region image layout to resolve nonce bootstrapping.
AI.result: Successful end-to-end message embedding and recovery on PNG images with deterministic success under correct conditions and failure otherwise; supports both text and image payloads with exact pixel-level recovery in image-in-image configuration.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides secure context-bound steganography where any deviation in required factors leads to silent rejection instead of partial disclosure, verified through experimental results on real quantum hardware.

title: Large-Scale Quantum Circuit Simulation on an Exascale System for QPU Benchmarking
summary: Recent advances in quantum computing have enabled the development of quantum processors with hundreds of qubits. However, noise continues to limit the amount of useful information that can be extracted from these systems, making it essential to identify the regime in which experimental outputs remain reliable. In this work, we benchmark Quantinuum Helios-1, a 98-qubit trapped-ion quantum processing unit, using the linear ramp quantum approximate optimization algorithm (LR-QAOA). To this end, we perform large-scale noiseless simulations on JUPITER, Europe's first exascale supercomputer, for circuits of up to 48 qubits and 3,384 two-qubit gates. These simulations, executed on 4,096 nodes equipped with 16,384 GH200 superchips and high-bandwidth CPU-GPU interconnects, provide a reference for validating experimental results at the edge of classical tractability. We find that, up to 48 qubits, Helios-1 remains in a noise-tolerant region, i.e., its samples cannot be clearly distinguished from those coming from a noiseless simulation. We then extend the analysis to larger system sizes using experimental data only, and apply a mean-of-means resampling procedure with a 3$σ$ threshold to determine whether the QPU output is statistically distinguishable from random sampling. This analysis identifies a regime of coherent performance up to 93 qubits (12,834 two-qubit gates), beyond which, at 95 qubits, the outputs become statistically indistinguishable from random sampling. These results demonstrate how exascale classical simulation can be used to validate quantum processors, and provide a quantitative boundary between noise-tolerant and random regimes in quantum processors.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks Quantinuum Helios-1, a 98-qubit trapped-ion quantum processor, using LR-QAOA. Noiseless simulations on JUPITER exascale supercomputer (up to 48 qubits, 3,384 two-qubit gates) show Helios-1 remains in a noise-tolerant region. Extended analysis using a mean-of-means resampling with 3σ threshold identifies coherent performance up to 93 qubits, beyond which outputs become statistically indistinguishable from random sampling.
AI.motivation: To identify the regime where experimental outputs from noisy quantum processors remain reliable, and to establish a quantitative boundary between noise-tolerant and random regimes in quantum processors.
AI.method: Benchmark Quantinuum Helios-1 using linear ramp QAOA, perform large-scale noiseless simulations on JUPITER exascale supercomputer (4,096 nodes, 16,384 GH200 superchips) for up to 48 qubits and 3,384 two-qubit gates, and apply a mean-of-means resampling procedure with a 3σ threshold for larger system sizes.
AI.result: Up to 48 qubits, Helios-1 samples cannot be clearly distinguished from noiseless simulations. Coherent performance is observed up to 93 qubits (12,834 two-qubit gates); at 95 qubits, outputs become statistically indistinguishable from random sampling.
AI.conclusion: Exascale classical simulation can effectively validate quantum processors, providing a quantitative boundary between noise-tolerant and random regimes.

title: A Multi-Level Integrity Evaluation Framework for Quantum Circuits under Controlled Anomaly Injection
summary:
Ensuring the integrity of quantum circuits is a significant challenge in the Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) era, where circuits are subject to compilation transformations, hardware constraints, and potential adversarial modifications. Existing validation approaches typically rely on either structural analysis or behavioral evaluation, leading to incomplete assessment of circuit correctness.
  In this work, we investigate the relationship between structural, interaction-level, and behavioral perspectives of circuit integrity, demonstrating that a single aspect of integrity is insufficient to guarantee circuit integrity; structural similarity alone does not ensure behavioral equivalence. To address this problem, we use a three-layer metric framework that combines the Structural Integrity Score (SIS), the Operational Integrity Score (OIS), and the Interaction Graph Semantic-Logical Score (IGS). SIS captures global structural properties, OIS quantifies behavioral divergence using Jensen-Shannon distance, and IGS models interaction patterns and dependencies in a pre-execution setting.
  Through controlled anomaly injection on benchmark quantum circuits, we demonstrate that each metric captures a different aspect of circuit deviation. In particular, structural blind-spot cases (SIS >= 0.95) reveal a clear limitation of structural analysis, where OIS detects anomalies in 93.85% of instances, while IGS detects 72.58%. These results highlight that the metrics provide complementary insights and that a single metric is insufficient for reliable circuit validation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a three-layer metric framework (SIS, OIS, IGS) for quantum circuit integrity validation, demonstrating that structural analysis alone is insufficient and that combining structural, behavioral, and interaction-level perspectives provides complementary insights, with OIS detecting 93.85% of structural blind-spot anomalies.
AI.motivation: Existing quantum circuit validation approaches rely on either structural analysis or behavioral evaluation in isolation, leading to incomplete assessments of circuit correctness. The paper aims to show that a single aspect of integrity is insufficient and that structural similarity alone does not guarantee behavioral equivalence.
AI.method: The authors develop a three-layer metric framework: Structural Integrity Score (SIS) for global structural properties, Operational Integrity Score (OIS) using Jensen-Shannon distance for behavioral divergence, and Interaction Graph Semantic-Logical Score (IGS) for pre-execution interaction patterns. Controlled anomaly injection on benchmark quantum circuits is used to evaluate the metrics.
AI.result: Each metric captures different aspects of circuit deviation. In structural blind-spot cases (SIS >= 0.95), OIS detects anomalies in 93.85% of instances, while IGS detects 72.58%. The metrics provide complementary insights, confirming that no single metric is sufficient for reliable circuit validation.
AI.conclusion: A multi-faceted approach combining structural, operational, and interaction-level metrics is necessary for robust quantum circuit integrity validation in the NISQ era, as single-aspect analysis leaves blind spots that can be exploited by adversarial modifications or overlooked by compilation transformations.

title: Solve Crude Oil Scheduling Problems by Using Quantum-Classical Hybrid Algorithms
summary: The optimization of front-end crude oil scheduling is a critical determinant of refinery profitability and operational stability. However, the coupling of discrete logistics events (e.g., vessel berthing) with continuous material flows (e.g., pipeline transfers) renders this problem an NP-hard Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) challenge, often intractable for classical solvers at industrial scales. This study proposes a novel hybrid quantum-classical framework to address these computational bottlenecks. We employ Benders Decomposition to decouple the monolithic model into a discrete Master Problem (MP) and a continuous Subproblem (SP). To exploit the search capabilities of quantum computing, the MP is reformulated as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) model and solved via a hybrid quantum solver, while the SP enforces mass balance and quality constraints through iterative optimality and feasibility cuts. Extensive experiments on 15 multi-scale instances demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms traditional metaheuristics (e.g., Genetic Algorithms, Tabu Search), reducing total operating costs by approximately 73--80% and achieving computational speeds comparable to state-of-the-art commercial solvers (Gurobi). By effectively leveraging global optimality cuts, the method overcomes the tendency of heuristic approaches to trap in local optima, providing a robust and scalable solution for complex refinery logistics.

AI.tldr: A hybrid quantum-classical framework using Benders Decomposition and QUBO formulation is proposed for front-end crude oil scheduling, outperforming metaheuristics by 73-80% in cost reduction while matching commercial solver speeds.
AI.motivation: Front-end crude oil scheduling is a critical determinant of refinery profitability and operational stability, but the coupling of discrete logistics with continuous material flows creates an NP-hard MILP challenge that is intractable for classical solvers at industrial scales.
AI.method: Benders Decomposition decouples the monolithic model into a discrete Master Problem (MP) and a continuous Subproblem (SP). The MP is reformulated as a QUBO model and solved via a hybrid quantum solver, while the SP enforces mass balance and quality constraints through iterative optimality and feasibility cuts.
AI.result: On 15 multi-scale instances, the framework reduces total operating costs by approximately 73-80% over traditional metaheuristics (Genetic Algorithms, Tabu Search) and achieves computational speeds comparable to Gurobi.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging global optimality cuts, the method overcomes local optima trapping common in heuristics, providing a robust and scalable solution for complex refinery logistics.

title: The Fock-Darwin-Darboux system: eigenstates, information entropies and dispersion-like measures
summary: The Fock-Darwin (FD) quantum system describes the motion on the plane of a charged particle under the action of an isotropic oscillator potential together with a perpendicular constant magnetic field. When the isotropic oscillator is suppressed, the FD system leads to the Landau Hamiltonian with infinitely degenerate Landau levels. The Fock-Darwin-Darboux (FDD) system is the generalisation of the FD system to a particle moving on the Darboux III space, which is a conformally flat surface with non-constant negative curvature. We present a systematic study of some information-theoretic entropy and dispersion-like measures for these quantum systems. Since both systems are exactly solvable, analytical expressions for Shannon, Rényi and Tsallis entropies, among others, can be obtained. We show that for the FD system, its information-theoretic measures are formally the same as the ones for the harmonic oscillator, provided a modified effective frequency depending on the magnetic field is introduced. In the FDD case, the nonlinear nature of the underlying manifold precludes the existence of a simple closed form for the wave-function on momentum space, which is numerically analysed. We compare the numerical behaviour of the different entropy measures and we analyse the interplay arising in the FDD system between the curvature parameter and the magnetic field. In particular, it is shown that the Landau system on the Darboux III space has no infinitely degenerate Landau levels.

AI.tldr: This paper studies information-theoretic entropy measures (Shannon, Rényi, Tsallis) for the Fock-Darwin (FD) quantum system and its generalization to the curved Darboux III space (FDD system). For FD, measures formally match the harmonic oscillator with a modified effective frequency. For FDD, the curved space breaks analytical solvability in momentum space and removes the infinite degeneracy of Landau levels.
AI.motivation: To systematically analyze information-theoretic entropy measures for the Fock-Darwin system and its curved-space generalization (FDD), and to understand how curvature and magnetic field interplay affect these measures and the degeneracy of Landau levels.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of Shannon, Rényi, and Tsallis entropies for the FD system using exact solvability, showing formal equivalence to the harmonic oscillator with an effective frequency. For the FDD system on the curved Darboux III space, numerical analysis of wave functions in momentum space is employed due to the lack of closed-form expressions.
AI.result: FD system's information-theoretic measures are formally identical to those of the harmonic oscillator with a modified effective frequency that depends on the magnetic field. In the FDD system, the Landau levels lose their infinite degeneracy, and the curvature parameter and magnetic field exhibit a nontrivial interplay that is numerically characterized.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that while the FD system's entropy measures mirror the harmonic oscillator (with magnetic field scaling the effective frequency), the curved Darboux III space fundamentally alters the FDD system's properties, eliminating infinite Landau level degeneracy and requiring numerical treatment for momentum-space quantities.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization by Quantum-Annealing-Inspired Algorithms
summary: Combinatorial optimization is widely regarded as a primary application for near-term quantum processors, although a definitive demonstration of the practical quantum advantage remains elusive. Recent studies have reported that both gate-based quantum circuits and quantum annealers can outperform state-of-the-art classical heuristics on multi-objective optimization (MO-MaxCut) problems. However, these studies did not fully account for the substantial pre- and post-processing overheads intrinsic to quantum solvers, leading to incomplete comparisons between quantum and classical approaches. In this work, we re-examine the same benchmark suite using GPU-based quantum-annealing-inspired algorithms (QAIAs), which, analogously to quantum processors, generate probabilistic samples and thus serve as formidable classical contenders. Our results show that QAIAs can sample candidate solutions approximately two orders of magnitude faster than previously studied quantum processors. In terms of end-to-end runtime, QAIAs also surpass industry-leading classical solvers, thereby establishing themselves as the superior performers among the quantum and classical solvers evaluated thus far for the MO-MaxCut instances.

AI.tldr: GPU-based quantum-annealing-inspired algorithms (QAIAs) solve MO-MaxCut problems ~100x faster than quantum processors, achieving superior end-to-end performance over both quantum and classical solvers.
AI.motivation: Prior claims of quantum advantage for multi-objective optimization (MO-MaxCut) did not account for pre- and post-processing overheads of quantum solvers, leaving incomplete comparisons.
AI.method: Re-examined MO-MaxCut benchmarks using GPU-based QAIAs, which generate probabilistic samples analogous to quantum processors, and compared end-to-end runtimes against both quantum processors and classical solvers.
AI.result: QAIAs sample candidate solutions approximately two orders of magnitude faster than previously studied quantum processors; QAIAs also surpass industry-leading classical solvers in end-to-end runtime.
AI.conclusion: QAIAs are currently the superior performers among both quantum and classical solvers evaluated for MO-MaxCut instances.

title: Fundamental Physics, Existential Risks and Human Futures
comment: Invited article for Phil. Trans. Roy. Soc. for the 25th anniversary of their millennium volume
summary: Over the past 25 years, I have been involved in some intriguing developments in the foundations of physics, exploring the quantum reality problem, the relationship between quantum theory and gravity and the interplay between consciousness and physical laws. These investigations make it plausible that we will find physics beyond quantum theory, potentially including both new evolution laws and new types of measurement. There is also a significant chance they could have potentially transformative impact on information processing and on the development of and our future with AI.

AI.tldr: Over 25 years, the author has explored foundations of physics—quantum reality, quantum-gravity links, and consciousness-physics interplay—suggesting that physics beyond quantum theory may emerge, with new evolution laws and measurement types, potentially transforming information processing and AI development.
AI.motivation: To investigate unresolved foundational issues in physics (quantum reality, quantum-gravity relationship, consciousness-physics connection) that suggest current quantum theory may be incomplete.
AI.method: Review of 25 years of personal research across three interconnected areas: the quantum reality problem, the quantum-gravity relationship, and the interplay between consciousness and physical laws.
AI.result: It is plausible that physics beyond quantum theory will be discovered, potentially including new evolution laws and new types of measurement, with possible transformative impacts on information processing and AI.
AI.conclusion: Foundational physics investigations may lead to extensions beyond quantum theory, with significant implications for future technologies including AI.

title: Neural and Tensor Networks in the Study of Quantum Annealing Processors
summary:
Quantum annealing targets low-energy solutions of Ising/QUBO problems, but reliable assessment requires more than best-energy comparisons. This dissertation develops a benchmarking framework for D-Wave quantum annealers that combines strong classical baselines, sampling and diversity metrics, and thermodynamic cost.
  Its first contribution, SpinGlassPEPS.jl, is a topology-aware tensor-network heuristic for optimization and sampling on Pegasus/Zephyr-like graphs. It maps Ising instances to local Potts clusters, represents the partition function with PEPS, and performs branch-and-bound search in probability space. Benchmarks show that it is a physically interpretable reference solver, though approximate contractions limit its competitiveness on the largest instances.
  The second contribution treats quantum annealers as effective thermal machines, relating success probability and solution quality to dissipation, entropy production, and effective temperature. Carefully placed pauses can improve performance while reducing thermodynamic cost, although longitudinal fields may become harmful in paused schedules.
  The thesis also introduces reinforcement-learning post-processing to improve returned samples and exact small-system simulations to probe annealing dynamics. Overall, it argues for quantum-annealing benchmarks that jointly measure algorithmic performance and physical expenditure.

AI.tldr: This dissertation develops a benchmarking framework for D-Wave quantum annealers combining classical baselines (SpinGlassPEPS.jl with tensor-network heuristics), sampling/diversity metrics, and thermodynamic cost analysis. It introduces RL-based post-processing and exact small-system simulations, arguing for benchmarks that jointly measure algorithmic performance and physical expenditure.
AI.motivation: Reliable assessment of quantum annealers requires more than best-energy comparisons; a comprehensive benchmarking framework is needed.
AI.method: (1) SpinGlassPEPS.jl: topology-aware tensor-network heuristic mapping Ising to Potts clusters, using PEPS for partition function and branch-and-bound search. (2) Thermodynamic analysis treating annealers as thermal machines. (3) RL-based post-processing and exact small-system simulations.
AI.result: SpinGlassPEPS.jl serves as physically interpretable reference solver but approximate contractions limit large-instance competitiveness. Pauses can improve performance while reducing thermodynamic cost; longitudinal fields may become harmful in paused schedules.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-annealing benchmarks should jointly measure algorithmic performance and physical expenditure (thermodynamic cost).

title: Random Number Generators in Advanced Optical Experiments: A Comparative Analysis of Semiclassical, Quantum, and Hybrid Architectures
summary: Random numbers sequences (RNSs) play a vital role in various scientific and engineering applications. They are critical to the integrity of classical and quantum cryptography, the accuracy of mathematical modeling and Monte Carlo simulations, and the core mechanics of applications in fields as diverse as gambling and statistical sampling. While the primary criteria for RNSs sources are their quality and generation rate, their integration into experimental designs is equally significant for many fundamental physical tests and applications. This work presents a comparative analysis of optical random number generation architectures, which can be seamlessly included into various advanced classical and quantum optical experimental schemes. In particular, we evaluate the trade-off between the high generation rate of an attenuated laser (a quasi-single-photon source) and the superior statistical quality of a heralded single-photon source operating at a much lower frequency. To overcome the limitations of each individual source, we propose and examine a novel hybrid architecture that utilizes their mixed radiation, enabling the generation of highquality RNSs at an enhanced rate. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the raw sequences generated by such a source can not only exhibit but, in some cases, even surpass the degree of randomness achieved by sequences processed through powerful randomness extractors.

AI.tldr: This paper compares optical random number generation architectures—attenuated laser (high rate, lower quality) vs. heralded single-photon source (high quality, low rate)—and proposes a hybrid architecture mixing both sources to achieve high-quality RNSs at enhanced rates, sometimes surpassing randomness extractors.
AI.motivation: RNSs are critical for cryptography, simulations, and fundamental physics tests; there is a need to balance generation rate and statistical quality in optical random number generation for integration into advanced experimental schemes.
AI.method: Comparative analysis of optical RNS architectures (attenuated laser vs. heralded single-photon source), followed by a proposed hybrid architecture that mixes radiation from both sources to enhance rate without compromising quality.
AI.result: The hybrid architecture generates high-quality RNSs at an enhanced rate; raw sequences from this source can match or surpass the randomness of sequences processed through powerful randomness extractors.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid optical source overcomes individual source limitations, offering a viable path toward high-rate, high-quality random number generation for advanced classical and quantum optics experiments.

title: Addressable Rydberg excitation in arrays of single neutral atoms with a strongly focused flat-top beam
summary: We present a method for generating a laser beam with flat intensity and phase profiles in the focal region where the beam interacts with neutral $^{87}$Rb atoms in an array of optical dipole traps. We synthesize the beam as a superposition of Hermite--Gaussian or Laguerre--Gaussian modes. Then we give analytical expressions for the coefficients of such a superposition, an analysis of beam propagation along the $z$ axis in the vicinity of the waist, and several other related theoretical issues. Rydberg two-qubit dynamics driven by this flat-top profile are analyzed through numerical solutions of the Lindblad master equation using our in-house Julia package. Beam preparation is demonstrated on a neutral-atom experimental platform. Measurements reveal a difference in the visibility of Rabi oscillations for addressed atoms compared with neighboring ones, confirming the effective spatial selectivity provided by the flat-top beam profile.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method to generate a flat-top laser beam using superposition of Hermite-Gaussian or Laguerre-Gaussian modes, analyzes its propagation, demonstrates its use in driving Rydberg two-qubit dynamics, and experimentally validates the beam's spatial selectivity in a neutral-atom optical dipole trap array.
AI.motivation: To achieve uniform intensity and phase profiles in the focal region for precise, spatially selective addressing of individual neutral atoms in an optical dipole trap array, which is critical for high-fidelity Rydberg two-qubit gates.
AI.method: The flat-top beam is synthesized as a superposition of Hermite-Gaussian or Laguerre-Gaussian modes with analytically derived coefficients. Beam propagation near the waist is analyzed theoretically. Rydberg two-qubit dynamics are simulated via Lindblad master equation using a custom Julia package. Experimental beam preparation and measurement of Rabi oscillation visibility contrast between addressed and neighboring atoms are performed on a neutral-atom platform.
AI.result: Analytical expressions for superposition coefficients are provided. Numerical simulations of Rydberg two-qubit dynamics under flat-top driving are shown. Experimental measurements confirm a difference in Rabi oscillation visibility between addressed atoms and their neighbors, demonstrating effective spatial selectivity of the flat-top beam profile.
AI.conclusion: The flat-top laser beam method enables spatially selective addressing of individual atoms in an array, which is beneficial for neutral-atom quantum computing architectures, particularly for Rydberg-based two-qubit operations.

title: All pure entangled states can lead to fully nonlocal correlations
summary: It is a well-established fact that some quantum correlations can be nonlocal, meaning that they cannot be described by a local hidden variable model. Certain quantum correlations have a form of nonlocality so strong that they cannot be reproduced even by models having an arbitrarily small local hidden variable component. These correlations are called fully nonlocal and lead to Bell inequalities in which the maximum quantum value saturates the non-signaling bound. A well-known example of this effect, which is also referred to as quantum pseudo-telepathy or all-versus-nothing proofs of nonlocality, is the quantum distribution fulfilling the Peres-Mermin square, in which the underlying state is a $4\times4$ dimensional maximally entangled state. Other examples of full nonlocality are known but, so far, all of them are for maximally entangled states and it is an open question whether maximal entanglement is necessary for full nonlocality. In this work, we first establish a link between full nonlocality and the concept of antidistinguishability of quantum states. We use this connection to show that in every bipartite $d\times d$ Hilbert space, with $d\geq3$, there are non-maximally entangled states that are fully nonlocal. In fact, we derive simple sufficient conditions for full nonlocality that are only based on the smallest and largest Schmidt coefficients. We also show that in every dimension there exist pure entangled states that do not exhibit full nonlocality. Finally, we show that all pure entangled states can be activated to show full nonlocality in the many-copy scenario.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a link between full nonlocality (quantum correlations that cannot be reproduced by any local hidden variable model, even with an arbitrarily small local component) and antidistinguishability of quantum states. Using this connection, the authors show that in bipartite d×d Hilbert spaces with d≥3, non-maximally entangled states can also be fully nonlocal, deriving sufficient conditions based on Schmidt coefficients. They also demonstrate that not all pure entangled states exhibit full nonlocality, but all such states can be activated to show full nonlocality in the many-copy scenario.
AI.motivation: It was an open question whether maximal entanglement is necessary for full nonlocality; this work aims to determine if non-maximally entangled states can also exhibit full nonlocality.
AI.method: The authors establish a formal link between full nonlocality and antidistinguishability of quantum states, then use this connection to derive sufficient conditions for full nonlocality based on the smallest and largest Schmidt coefficients of bipartite states.
AI.result: In bipartite d×d Hilbert spaces with d≥3, there exist non-maximally entangled states that are fully nonlocal. Sufficient conditions for full nonlocality are derived solely from Schmidt coefficients. Not all pure entangled states exhibit full nonlocality, but all can be activated in the many-copy regime.
AI.conclusion: Maximal entanglement is not necessary for full nonlocality. The work provides new criteria for identifying fully nonlocal states and shows that full nonlocality can be activated via multiple copies.

title: Probabilistic Condition, Decision and Path Coverage of Circuit-based Quantum Programs
summary: Coverage criteria play a central role in assessing test adequacy in classical software, yet their effectiveness for quantum programs remains poorly understood and largely unexplored. In this paper, we propose six quantum-tailored criteria - condition, decision, and path coverage, and their probabilistic variants - adapted from their classical counterparts. We present QaCoCo, a tool that computes these criteria for circuit-based quantum programs. We empirically evaluate these criteria on a large and diverse set of 540 circuits and analyze the coverage achieved. Our results show that while circuits frequently achieve high condition and decision coverage (97.56% and 97.63%, on average), path coverage remains limited (71.84%), particularly in the presence of multi-controlled gates, which induce extreme path explosion and coverage imbalance. Moreover, to account for the probabilistic nature of quantum circuits, we introduce probabilistic coverage, which augments structural coverage with a confidence measure (88.87%, 88.65%, and 37.18% for condition, decision, and path coverage, respectively, on average). Finally, through mutation testing, we find weak or no correlation between fault detection and structural coverage, consistent with observations in classical computing.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes six quantum-tailored coverage criteria (condition, decision, path coverage, and their probabilistic variants) for circuit-based quantum programs, implemented via the QaCoCo tool. Evaluated on 540 circuits, results show high condition/decision coverage (97.56%/97.63%) but low path coverage (71.84%), especially with multi-controlled gates. Probabilistic variants yield lower averages (88.87%/88.65%/37.18%). Mutation testing reveals weak/no correlation between coverage and fault detection.
AI.motivation: Coverage criteria are foundational for assessing test adequacy in classical software, but their applicability and effectiveness for quantum programs remain largely unexplored and poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors propose six quantum-tailored coverage criteria (condition, decision, path coverage and their probabilistic variants), implement them in a tool called QaCoCo, and empirically evaluate on 540 quantum circuits. Mutation testing is used to assess correlation between coverage and fault detection.
AI.result: High condition/decision coverage (97.56%/97.63% avg) but low path coverage (71.84% avg), exacerbated by multi-controlled gates. Probabilistic coverage yields 88.87%, 88.65%, and 37.18% for condition, decision, and path coverage. Weak/no correlation found between structural coverage and fault detection via mutation testing.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-tailored coverage criteria are feasible and reveal distinct patterns compared to classical software, but like classical computing, structural coverage alone is insufficient for assessing fault detection effectiveness.

title: Tikhonov-regularised projected gradient flow for equality-constrained bilinear quantum control
summary: We study a projection-type gradient flow for equality-constrained maximisation of a smooth bilinear control objective on $\mathcal{H}=L^2(0,T;\mathbb{R})$, eliminating Lagrange multipliers through an $(M{+}1)\times(M{+}1)$ moving Gram matrix $Γ(s)_{\ell\ell'}=\int_0^T S(t)\,c_\ell(s,t)\,c_{\ell'}(s,t)\,\mathrm{d}t$. The flow generates monotonic ascent in continuous time but becomes unstable on discretisation; existing implementations rely on heuristic step-size safeguards lacking rigorous justification. We close this gap by replacing $Γ$ with $Γ_{\varepsilon}:=Γ+\varepsilon^{2}I$ and prove: (i) an exact spectral identity giving $κ(Γ_{\varepsilon})=(σ_{\max}^{2}+\varepsilon^{2})/(σ_{\min}^{2}+\varepsilon^{2})$; (ii) objective monotonicity $\mathrm{d}J/\mathrm{d}s\ge 0$ for all $\varepsilon\ge 0$; (iii) constraint drift $|h_{m}-C_{m}|=\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{2})$ with a computable prefactor; (iv) convergence of the regularised trajectory to the unregularised one in $L^{2}(0,T)$ at rate $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{2})$ under uniform invertibility of $Γ$; and (v) a discrete CFL criterion $Δs\,G\,\|Γ_{\varepsilon}^{-1}\|\leα<2$ guaranteeing objective monotonicity of the forward-Euler scheme up to $\mathcal{O}(Δs^{2})$ local truncation error. The theory is validated on a three-level bilinear benchmark for all-optical Bell-state preparation, where $κ(Γ)\in[10^{9},10^{11}]$, the predicted $\varepsilon^{2}$ rate is confirmed over eight decades, and moderate regularisation eliminates step rejections and reduces constraint drift by more than an order of magnitude at unchanged final fidelity.

AI.tldr: A regularized projection-type gradient flow for bilinear control is proposed, replacing the ill-conditioned Gram matrix Γ with Γ_ε = Γ + ε²I. The regularization ensures spectral conditioning, monotonic ascent, bounded constraint drift (O(ε²)), and convergence of regularized to unregularized trajectories (O(ε²) in L²). A discrete CFL condition guarantees monotonicity of forward-Euler. Validated on a Bell-state preparation benchmark where κ(Γ)~10⁹–10¹¹, the method eliminates step rejections and reduces drift by an order of magnitude at unchanged fidelity.
AI.motivation: Existing projection-type gradient flows for equality-constrained bilinear control produce monotonic ascent in continuous time but become unstable upon discretisation, forcing practitioners to rely on heuristic step-size safeguards without rigorous justification.
AI.method: Replace the moving Gram matrix Γ with Γ_ε = Γ + ε²I; prove spectral identity for condition number; analyse monotonicity, constraint drift, trajectory convergence, and forward-Euler stability with a CFL-type condition.
AI.result: Proved: (i) exact spectral identity for κ(Γ_ε); (ii) dJ/ds ≥ 0 for all ε ≥ 0; (iii) constraint drift |h_m - C_m| = O(ε²) with computable prefactor; (iv) L² convergence of regularised to unregularised trajectory at O(ε²) rate; (v) discrete CFL condition Δs·G·‖Γ_ε⁻¹‖ < 2 guaranteeing forward-Euler monotonicity up to O(Δs²) truncation error. Experiments on a three-level bilinear benchmark (κ(Γ) ∈ [10⁹,10¹¹]) confirm the ε² rate over eight decades, eliminate step rejections, and reduce drift >10× at unchanged final fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed regularisation provides a theoretically grounded, practically effective remedy for ill-conditioning in projection-type gradient flows for bilinear control, with rigorous guarantees and excellent empirical performance.

title: Emergence of $π$ from Equatorial Quantum Localization
summary: We present a genuinely non-radial quantum-mechanical route by which $π$ emerges from equatorial localization on the sphere. For the highest-weight branch of spherical harmonics, this localization is captured by a natural geometric rigidity index, whose exact finite-quantum-number value is a Wallis partial product. The mechanism is realized in two settings: the standard rigid rotor and the surface sector of a thin spherical shell, where radial freezing reduces the dynamics to the same angular problem. In the large-quantum-number limit, the probability cloud collapses toward the equator, the rigidity index approaches its classical value, and the Wallis formula is recovered through the correspondence principle. The result shows that Wallis-type structures in quantum mechanics can arise as exact signatures of semiclassical localization encoded by a simple geometric observable.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that π emerges from equatorial localization of spherical harmonics, with a rigidity index given by Wallis partial products, recovering the Wallis formula via the correspondence principle.
