AI.motivation: To demonstrate a non-radial quantum-mechanical route for π emergence via equatorial localization on a sphere, and to connect Wallis structures to semiclassical localization.
AI.method: Analyzes the highest-weight branch of spherical harmonics using a geometric rigidity index (Wallis partial product), applied to the rigid rotor and thin spherical shell systems, with large-quantum-number asymptotics.
AI.result: The rigidity index equals a Wallis partial product exactly for finite quantum numbers; in the classical limit, the probability cloud collapses to the equator and the Wallis formula is recovered.
AI.conclusion: Wallis-type structures in quantum mechanics can arise as exact signatures of semiclassical localization encoded by a simple geometric observable.

title: The temperature dependent geometric phase
summary: There exists a geometric phase for a quantum state during the adiabatic evolution of the system. If the adiabatic procedure happens between the system and the environment interacting with it similar to Born-Oppenheimer (BO) approximation, we can introduce a temperature into the environment, which can be regarded as in an equilibrium state. Then a temperature-dependent geometric phase can be obtained for the system, which originates from the Abelian gauge potential induced by the BO approximation. This gauge potential contributes to the effective potential of the system, which is temperature dependent, too. Finally, we demonstrate them using an example of H_2^+ ion system.

AI.tldr: The paper derives a temperature-dependent geometric phase for a quantum system undergoing adiabatic evolution in contact with a thermal bath (equilibrium environment), using the Born-Oppenheimer approximation. This phase arises from an Abelian gauge potential that also yields a temperature-dependent effective potential, demonstrated on the H₂⁺ ion system.
AI.motivation: To extend the concept of geometric (Berry) phase from isolated adiabatic quantum systems to open systems interacting with a finite-temperature environment, by incorporating temperature via the Born-Oppenheimer approximation framework.
AI.method: Apply the Born-Oppenheimer approximation to treat the system-environment interaction, assume the environment is in thermal equilibrium at temperature T, and derive the Abelian gauge potential induced by the BO approximation. Compute the temperature-dependent geometric phase and effective potential from this gauge structure.
AI.result: A temperature-dependent geometric phase emerges from the Abelian gauge potential induced by the BO approximation; the effective potential of the system also acquires temperature dependence. These are demonstrated explicitly for the H₂⁺ molecular ion system.
AI.conclusion: Geometric phase in adiabatic quantum systems can be generalized to finite-temperature open systems via the BO approximation, yielding a temperature-dependent phase and effective potential, with the H₂⁺ ion as a concrete example.

title: Least constraint approach to non-relativistic quantum mechanics
summary: We formulate a variational principle for non-relativistic quantum mechanics inspired by Gauss's principle of least constraint. We define a quantum constraint functional as the probability-weighted square deviation between the actual motion and the unconstrained motion that would arise from external forces alone. In this functional, the quantum potential plays the role of an intrinsic constraint that modifies the acceleration. Minimizing this quantum constraint functional with respect to the acceleration field yields the quantum Euler equations, which together with the continuity equation are equivalent to the Schrödinger equation. The principle is instantaneous and provides a differential characterization of quantum evolution. We demonstrate that this formulation is not a mere rewriting of existing dynamics: it provides a unified and technically economical treatment of geometric constraints and velocity-dependent dissipative forces, neither of which admits a straightforward global variational formulation. Potential applications to a broad range of quantum phenomena are also indicated.

AI.tldr: A variational principle for non-relativistic quantum mechanics based on Gauss's principle of least constraint is formulated, introducing a quantum constraint functional that yields quantum Euler equations equivalent to the Schrödinger equation, with applications to geometric constraints and dissipative forces.
AI.motivation: To develop a variational principle for quantum mechanics that can handle geometric constraints and velocity-dependent dissipative forces, which do not admit straightforward global variational formulations.
AI.method: The approach defines a quantum constraint functional as the probability-weighted square deviation between actual motion and unconstrained motion, where the quantum potential acts as an intrinsic constraint modifying acceleration. Minimizing this functional with respect to the acceleration field yields quantum Euler equations, which together with the continuity equation are equivalent to the Schrödinger equation.
AI.result: The variational principle produces quantum Euler equations equivalent to the Schrödinger equation. It provides a unified and technically economical treatment of geometric constraints and velocity-dependent dissipative forces.
AI.conclusion: The formulation is not a mere rewriting of existing dynamics; it offers a differential (instantaneous) characterization of quantum evolution with broad applicability to quantum phenomena involving constraints and dissipation.

title: Nonclassical traits in multi-copy state discrimination
summary: Quantum state discrimination is a fundamental information processing task that serves as a building block for numerous applications and provides implications at the foundational level. In this work, we consider minimum error discrimination of multi-copy states, where instead of preparing a single system we assume that multiple instances of the same state are prepared. Now the discrimination allows for measurements from multiple parties with different measurement strategies varying from global measurement strategy to ones restricted to different forms of local operations and classical communication strategies. By comparing the average success probabilities in quantum and classical cases, we find a qubit strategy that outperforms all the bit strategies. However, we find that there are other bit-like operational theories which can outperform the best qubit strategies even with a classical measurement strategy and we are able to identify instances of different theories where different measurement strategies are optimal. In this way, we are able to find instances of nonlocality without entanglement as well as provide general bounds for bit-like operational theories.

AI.tldr: This paper studies minimum error discrimination of multi-copy quantum states under various measurement strategies (global, LOCC, etc.), finding that certain qubit strategies outperform classical bit strategies, but some bit-like operational theories can surpass qubit strategies even with classical measurements. It identifies cases of nonlocality without entanglement and provides general bounds for bit-like theories.
AI.motivation: To investigate minimum error discrimination of multi-copy quantum states across different measurement strategies (global vs. local operations and classical communication), and to compare quantum and classical performance limits.
AI.method: Comparing average success probabilities of quantum (qubit) and classical (bit) strategies, as well as examining bit-like operational theories under various measurement strategies (global, LOCC, classical).
AI.result: A qubit strategy outperforms all bit strategies; however, some bit-like operational theories with classical measurement strategies can outperform the best qubit strategies. Instances of nonlocality without entanglement are identified.
AI.conclusion: Different operational theories exhibit optimal performance under different measurement strategies, and general bounds for bit-like operational theories are established, revealing nonlocality without entanglement.

title: Hardware-Efficient Hamiltonian Simulation via Trotter-Initialized Variational Optimization with Native Placement
summary: Compiling time-evolution operators of the form $U(t)=e^{-iHt}$ into hardware-native gate sequences is a central bottleneck for digital quantum simulation on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices. Generic transpilation treats $U(t)$ as an arbitrary unitary, discarding the structure of Hamiltonian dynamics and producing circuits whose depth exceeds hardware coherence limits. We introduce a structure-aware compilation framework that treats product-formula decompositions as synthesis primitives rather than simulation approximations. The method combines (i) native placement of Hamiltonian terms onto the hardware coupling map, (ii) adaptive selection of Trotter blocks via a greedy discretization procedure, and (iii) variational refinement using a Trotter-initialized ansatz. Across Heisenberg, Ising, and XY models with $n=3$--$8$ qubits, the compiled circuits achieve fidelities $F>0.996$ with approximately linear scaling in the number of entangling gates, while generic synthesis produces circuits that are orders of magnitude deeper. On IBM Torino hardware, we observe a regime in which shorter approximate circuits outperform deeper exact decompositions: a 27-CX circuit achieves higher hardware fidelity ($F_{\mathrm{hw}}=0.987$) than a 187-CX exact circuit. These results demonstrate that, in the NISQ regime, structure-aware approximate compilation can outperform exact structure-agnostic synthesis, providing a practical pathway for executing Hamiltonian dynamics without requiring pulse-level control.

AI.tldr: A structure-aware compilation framework that treats product-formula decompositions as synthesis primitives outperforms exact generic transpilation on NISQ devices, achieving fidelities >0.996 with near-linear gate scaling and demonstrating that shorter approximate circuits (27-CX) can achieve higher hardware fidelity than deeper exact circuits (187-CX) on IBM Torino hardware.
AI.motivation: Compiling time-evolution operators into hardware-native gate sequences is a central bottleneck for digital quantum simulation on NISQ devices, as generic transpilation discards Hamiltonian structure and produces circuits exceeding hardware coherence limits.
AI.method: A structure-aware compilation framework combining (i) native placement of Hamiltonian terms onto hardware coupling maps, (ii) adaptive selection of Trotter blocks via greedy discretization, and (iii) variational refinement using a Trotter-initialized ansatz.
AI.result: On Heisenberg, Ising, and XY models (n=3-8 qubits), circuits achieve fidelities F>0.996 with approximately linear scaling in entangling gates. On IBM Torino, a 27-CX approximate circuit achieved F_hw=0.987 vs a 187-CX exact circuit, demonstrating shorter approximate circuits can outperform deeper exact decompositions.
AI.conclusion: In the NISQ regime, structure-aware approximate compilation can outperform exact structure-agnostic synthesis, providing a practical pathway for executing Hamiltonian dynamics without requiring pulse-level control.

title: Parameterized Quantum Circuits as Feature Maps: Representation Quality and Readout Effects in Multispectral Land-Cover Classification
summary: We investigate variational quantum classifiers (VQCs) for land-cover classification from multispectral satellite imagery, adopting a feature-map perspective in which the quantum circuit defines a nonlinear data embedding while the readout determines how this representation is exploited. Using the EuroSAT-MS dataset, we perform a systematic one-vs-one evaluation across all class pairs under a controlled experimental protocol, comparing classical baselines (logistic regression, SVMs, neural networks) with VQCs employing both linear readout and quantum-kernel SVM strategies. Our results show that, while VQCs with linear readout do not outperform strong classical baselines such as RBF-SVM, the same trained quantum feature map can significantly improve performance when reused within a kernel-based decision framework. A qubit-count sweep further reveals saturation effects consistent with the mismatch between exponential Hilbert space dimension and linear parameter scaling. Overall, our findings highlight that the effectiveness of quantum models depends critically on the interplay between representation and readout, and that meaningful gains may arise from combining learned quantum feature maps with classical decision mechanisms rather than seeking direct replacement of classical models.

AI.tldr: Variational quantum classifiers (VQCs) for land-cover classification using satellite imagery show that quantum feature maps with linear readout don't outperform classical RBF-SVM, but combining learned quantum embeddings with quantum-kernel SVM yields improvements, with saturation effects observed due to exponential Hilbert space vs. linear parameter scaling.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether variational quantum classifiers can improve land-cover classification from multispectral satellite imagery, and to understand the interplay between quantum representation (feature map) and readout strategy in determining model effectiveness.
AI.method: Systematic one-vs-one evaluation on the EuroSAT-MS dataset across all class pairs; compared classical baselines (logistic regression, SVMs, neural networks) with VQCs using both linear readout and quantum-kernel SVM strategies; performed qubit-count sweep to study scaling behavior.
AI.result: VQCs with linear readout do not outperform RBF-SVM baselines, but the same trained quantum feature map significantly improves performance when reused within a kernel-based decision framework; qubit-count sweep reveals saturation effects due to mismatch between exponential Hilbert space dimension and linear parameter scaling.
AI.conclusion: The effectiveness of quantum models depends critically on the interplay between representation and readout; meaningful gains arise from combining learned quantum feature maps with classical decision mechanisms rather than seeking direct replacement of classical models.

title: Towards Quantum Optimised Malware Containment
summary: The containment of malware in computing networks may be naturally formulated as a network influence minimisation problem, in which one seeks to limit the expected spread of an infection while balancing the operational cost of disabling network connections. Classical approaches often rely on Monte Carlo simulation of stochastic diffusion processes and greedy optimisation over candidate edge removals, resulting in significant computational overhead due to repeated influence evaluations. In this work, we propose a hybrid quantum approach which combines Quantum Amplitude Estimation (QAE) and Grover Minimum Finding (GMF) to provide quadratic improvements in both the estimation and optimisation components of the problem. Specifically, QAE replaces classical Monte Carlo simulation, reducing the sampling complexity of influence estimation from $O(1/\varepsilon^2)$ to $O(1/\varepsilon)$ for a target additive error $\varepsilon \ll 1$, while GMF reduces the number of candidate evaluations required to identify optimal edge removals from $O(|E_C|)$ to $O(\sqrt{|E_C|})$. We present a formal problem definition, describe the construction of the corresponding quantum oracles, and analyse the resulting complexity improvements under standard oracle assumptions. Preliminary experiments, including classical simulation of QAE and small-scale execution of Grover search on real quantum hardware, support the expected theoretical scaling. While practical implementation at scale requires fault-tolerant quantum devices, our results demonstrate that quantum algorithms offer a promising long-term direction for accelerating stochastic network optimisation problems such as malware containment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hybrid quantum approach combining Quantum Amplitude Estimation and Grover Minimum Finding to achieve quadratic speedups in both influence estimation and edge removal optimisation for malware containment in networks, replacing classical Monte Carlo and greedy methods.
AI.motivation: Classical approaches to malware containment suffer from high computational overhead due to repeated Monte Carlo simulations of stochastic diffusion and greedy optimisation over candidate edge removals.
AI.method: A hybrid quantum algorithm that uses Quantum Amplitude Estimation (QAE) to reduce influence estimation complexity from O(1/ε²) to O(1/ε), and Grover Minimum Finding (GMF) to reduce candidate edge evaluation from O(|E_C|) to O(√|E_C|).
AI.result: Preliminary experiments, including classical simulation of QAE and small-scale Grover search on real quantum hardware, support the expected theoretical quadratic speedups.
AI.conclusion: Quantum algorithms offer a promising long-term direction for accelerating stochastic network optimisation problems like malware containment, though practical large-scale implementation requires fault-tolerant quantum devices.

title: Observation of Non-Markovian Evolution of Tripartite Quantum Steering
summary: The memory effects in open quantum systems can induce information backflow and revive quantum correlations, thereby providing a powerful way to protect and recover useful quantum resources in realistic noisy environments. However, such dynamics remains experimentally unexplored in multipartite quantum steering. Here we observe different non-Markovian evolution of tripartite quantum steering using Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger-type mixed states, covering both death and revival processes. In particular, we experimentally demonstrate the more intricate asymmetric steering structure of tripartite quantum steering through different bipartitions, which do not arise in bipartite systems. Our results provide foundational insights into the hierarchical and directional structures in multipartite quantum steering, and highlight its potential as a useful resource for asymmetric quantum information processing.

AI.tldr: This paper experimentally observes non-Markovian dynamics of tripartite quantum steering using GHZ-type mixed states, demonstrating death and revival processes and asymmetric steering structures across different bipartitions that do not exist in bipartite systems.
AI.motivation: To experimentally explore the non-Markovian evolution of multipartite quantum steering, which has remained experimentally unexplored despite the theoretical potential of memory effects to protect and recover quantum resources in noisy environments.
AI.method: Experimental observation of non-Markovian dynamics using Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-type mixed states, analyzing tripartite quantum steering across different bipartitions to reveal asymmetric steering structures.
AI.result: Demonstrated both death and revival processes of tripartite quantum steering, and revealed intricate asymmetric steering structures through different bipartitions that are unique to multipartite systems.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide foundational insights into hierarchical and directional structures in multipartite quantum steering, highlighting its potential as a resource for asymmetric quantum information processing.

title: Delayed Choice Phenomena in the Projection Evolution Model
summary: In the Schrödinger evolution of a quantum state time enters as a real parameter representing the coordinate. In a more consistent approach time should be defined as a quantum observable, with the evolution taking place in a four-dimensional spacetime. This is possible in the projection evolution model in which the wave function is defined in both space and time. This allows to construct the time operator and discuss the temporal structure of quantum processes. In this paper we discuss a photon travelling through a Mach-Zehnder interferometer, focusing the description on the temporal profile of the wave function. We show that in this approach the delayed-choice experiments can be explained by the temporal overlap of the photon and the devices in the interferometer.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes treating time as a quantum observable rather than a parameter in quantum mechanics, using the projection evolution model. It analyzes a photon in a Mach-Zehnder interferometer, showing that delayed-choice experiments can be explained by temporal overlap between the photon and interferometer devices.
AI.motivation: Time is traditionally treated as a real parameter in Schrödinger evolution, but a more consistent approach should define time as a quantum observable within four-dimensional spacetime. This work aims to demonstrate the projection evolution model's capability to construct a time operator and explain temporal quantum processes.
AI.method: The authors apply the projection evolution model, where the wave function is defined in both space and time, allowing the construction of a time operator. They analyze a photon traveling through a Mach-Zehnder interferometer, focusing on the temporal profile of the wave function.
AI.result: The delayed-choice experiments can be explained by the temporal overlap of the photon and the devices in the interferometer, using the temporal profile of the wave function.
AI.conclusion: Treating time as a quantum observable in the projection evolution model provides a consistent framework for explaining quantum processes, including delayed-choice experiments, through temporal overlap considerations.

title: Optical squeezing mediated by levitated oscillators at their quantum ground state
summary: We demonstrate optical squeezing below the shot-noise level generated through the interaction of an optical cavity field with two center-of-mass modes of a levitated nanoparticle, simultaneously cooled to occupation numbers well below unity. By analyzing the quadrature fluctuations of the cavity output through heterodyne detection, we resolve the full spectral covariance matrix of the optical field and map regions of sub-shot-noise squeezing as a function of detection phase and frequency. Operating in the resolved sideband and strong coupling regime where mechanical modes hybridize with the optical mode, we observe consistent squeezing in the band 70-95 kHz with a lowest variance of 0.98 (2$\%$ below vacuum fluctuations). We thus demonstrate optical squeezing mediated by multiple mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground state, bridging mechanical quantum control with non-classical light and establishing levitated optomechanics as a platform for multimode quantum interactions.

AI.tldr: Optical squeezing below shot-noise level is achieved using two center-of-mass modes of a levitated nanoparticle, both cooled to their quantum ground state, in the resolved sideband and strong coupling regime. The lowest observed variance is 0.98 (2% below vacuum fluctuations) in the 70-95 kHz band.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate optical squeezing mediated by multiple mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground state, bridging mechanical quantum control with non-classical light and establishing levitated optomechanics as a platform for multimode quantum interactions.
AI.method: A levitated nanoparticle is trapped in an optical cavity, with two center-of-mass modes simultaneously cooled to occupation numbers below unity. Heterodyne detection is used to analyze quadrature fluctuations of the cavity output, resolving the full spectral covariance matrix and mapping sub-shot-noise squeezing regions as a function of detection phase and frequency.
AI.result: Consistent squeezing observed in the 70-95 kHz band with a lowest variance of 0.98 (2% below vacuum fluctuations).
AI.conclusion: Optical squeezing mediated by multiple mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground state has been demonstrated, extending levitated optomechanics into multimode quantum interactions and non-classical light generation.

title: Chip-to-chip entanglement distribution over 80-km multicore fiber link
summary: Long-range quantum entanglement is essential for building large-scale quantum networks and unconditionally secure cryptographic systems based on quantum key distribution (QKD). While photonic integrated circuits offer a highly scalable platform, the fragility of phase coherence between spatial modes has prevented the distribution of path-encoded entanglement over long distances. Here, we report chip-to-chip distribution of path-encoded entangled states over 80 km between fully integrated silicon photonic transmitter and receiver chips. Telecom-band entangled photon pairs are generated via spontaneous four-wave mixing in on-chip spiral waveguides and distributed between chips over a dual-core, actively stabilized fiber link. Upon distribution, we measure a Bell state fidelity of $85.7 \pm 0.2 \%$. Implementing the BBM92 protocol with the same source, we obtain a secure key rate of 2.03 bit/s in the infinite-key regime. These results establish silicon photonic chips as a viable platform for long-distance path-encoded entanglement-based quantum key distribution, paving the way toward scalable, device-independent quantum networks.

AI.tldr: Researchers demonstrated chip-to-chip distribution of path-encoded entanglement over 80 km using fully integrated silicon photonic chips, achieving 85.7% Bell state fidelity and a secure key rate of 2.03 bit/s via the BBM92 QKD protocol.
AI.motivation: Long-range quantum entanglement is essential for quantum networks and secure QKD, but phase coherence fragility between spatial modes has prevented path-encoded entanglement distribution over long distances, motivating the need for a scalable integrated photonic solution.
AI.method: Telecom-band entangled photon pairs were generated via spontaneous four-wave mixing in on-chip spiral waveguides, distributed between silicon photonic transmitter and receiver chips over a dual-core actively stabilized fiber link spanning 80 km, and the BBM92 QKD protocol was implemented using the same source.
AI.result: Bell state fidelity of 85.7 ± 0.2% was measured after distribution, and a secure key rate of 2.03 bit/s was obtained in the infinite-key regime using the BBM92 protocol.
AI.conclusion: Silicon photonic chips are established as a viable platform for long-distance path-encoded entanglement-based QKD, paving the way toward scalable, device-independent quantum networks.

title: Fault-Tolerant Resource Comparison of Qudit and Qubit Encodings for Diagonal Quadratic Operators
summary: Finite local Hilbert-space truncations arise naturally in quantum simulations of lattice field theories and motivate qudit encodings, but their fault-tolerant advantage over qubit encodings remains unclear. We compare the non-Clifford cost of implementing quadratic diagonal evolutions, exemplified by $U=e^{-itφ_x^2}$ in a uniform field-amplitude discretization of a real scalar field, using either one logical $d$-level qudit or $n_b=\lceil \log_2 d\rceil$ logical qubits. We analyze two standard settings: product-formula simulation and LCU/block encoding, taking the resource metric to be the number of non-Clifford gates after synthesis into a discrete logical gate set. Because tight synthesis bounds for general single-qudit rotations are not known, we express the qudit constructions in terms of embedded two-level $SU(2)$ rotations and derive explicit finite-$d$ break-even conditions for their synthesis cost; these serve as compiler targets for when qudit encodings can outperform the qubit baseline. Within the constructive models studied here, product-formula implementations would require an exponentially stronger per-primitive synthesis advantage for qudits to win asymptotically, while in the LCU setting the qubit encoding is asymptotically cheaper in $d$. Nevertheless, the finite-$d$ threshold analysis identifies low dimensional regions in which qudits can yield meaningful constant-factor savings, particularly for LCU-based implementations. As a secondary analysis of the LCU construction, we use an idealized negligible-overhead qubit-qudit code-switching model to give an absolute $T$-count comparison, and reinterpret the savings as an allowable per-switch overhead budget.

AI.tldr: This paper compares the non-Clifford gate cost of implementing quadratic diagonal evolutions using qudit vs. qubit encodings in quantum simulations of lattice field theories, finding that qudits offer limited finite-dimensional advantages in specific settings (especially LCU-based) but qubits are asymptotically cheaper.
AI.motivation: Finite local Hilbert-space truncations arise in quantum simulations of lattice field theories and motivate qudit encodings, but their fault-tolerant advantage over qubit encodings remains unclear.
AI.method: The authors compare the non-Clifford cost of implementing quadratic diagonal evolutions (e.g., U=e^{-itφ_x^2}) using either one logical d-level qudit or n_b=⌈log₂ d⌉ logical qubits. They analyze two settings: product-formula simulation and LCU/block encoding. Qudit constructions are expressed via embedded two-level SU(2) rotations with explicit finite-d break-even conditions.
AI.result: Product-formula implementations would require an exponentially stronger per-primitive synthesis advantage for qudits to win asymptotically; in the LCU setting, the qubit encoding is asymptotically cheaper in d. Finite-d threshold analysis identifies low-dimensional regions where qudits yield meaningful constant-factor savings, particularly for LCU-based implementations.
AI.conclusion: Within the constructive models studied, qudit encodings do not offer asymptotic advantage over qubit encodings for this class of evolutions, but can provide constant-factor savings in low-dimensional regimes, especially in LCU settings and under favorable code-switching overhead budgets.

title: HyPulse: A Pulse Synthesis Framework for Hybrid Qubit-Oscillator Gates on Trapped-Ion Platform
comment: 10 pages, 6 figures. Submitted to IEEE QCE 2026
summary: As hybrid qubit-oscillator algorithm development and trapped-ion hardware demonstrations advance in parallel, there is a lack of a compilation layer connecting the two at the pulse level in the vertical software stack. While qubit gate control and pulse synthesis are well-established, the translation of hybrid qubit-oscillator primitives to the pulse level has not been systematically addressed. This gap is further compounded by the inherently continuous parametric nature of such gates. Each distinct parameter value defines a physically unique operation requiring independent pulse optimization, making static pre-compilation strategies inapplicable. To fill this gap, we present HyPulse, a hardware-aware pulse synthesis and generation framework, which contributes a two-phase architecture decoupling pulse discovery from circuit assembly. An offline optimization engine populates a content-addressed cache of high-fidelity primitives: If a pulse for a given gate, parameter, and device specification already exists in the library, it is retrieved instantly; otherwise the optimizer synthesizes, hashes, and caches it automatically. An online assembler then constructs circuit-specific pulse programs ready to drive trapped-ion hardware control systems via DAX/ARTIQ (Duke) and JaqalPaw/QSCOUT (Sandia), trapped-ion pulse execution backends.

AI.tldr: HyPulse is a hardware-aware pulse synthesis framework that bridges the gap between hybrid qubit-oscillator algorithm primitives and trapped-ion pulse-level control by decoupling offline pulse discovery (with content-addressed caching) from online circuit assembly, supporting DAX/ARTIQ and JaqalPaw/QSCOUT backends.
AI.motivation: There is no existing compilation layer connecting hybrid qubit-oscillator algorithm primitives to pulse-level control in trapped-ion hardware, and the continuous parametric nature of these gates makes static pre-compilation infeasible.
AI.method: A two-phase architecture: (1) offline optimization engine that synthesizes, hashes, and caches high-fidelity pulses using a content-addressed library; (2) online assembler that constructs circuit-specific pulse programs for trapped-ion backends (DAX/ARTIQ and JaqalPaw/QSCOUT).
AI.result: HyPulse enables instant retrieval of cached pulse primitives for known gate-parameter-device combinations, and automatic synthesis/caching for unseen ones, providing a systematic pulse-level compilation pathway for hybrid qubit-oscillator circuits.
AI.conclusion: HyPulse fills the missing pulse-level compilation layer for hybrid qubit-oscillator systems with a decoupled two-phase design that handles continuous parametric gates via dynamic caching, and is compatible with multiple trapped-ion pulse execution backends.

title: Classical simulation of free-fermionic dynamics and quantum chemistry with magic input
summary: Establishing the precise computational boundary between classically tractable fermionic systems and those capable of genuine quantum advantage is a central challenge in quantum simulation. While injecting non-Gaussian ``magic" inputs into free-fermion circuits is widely expected to generate intractable complexity, we identify a physically motivated intermediate regime. Supported by rigorous bounds and numerical evidence, we show that for a class of paired non-Gaussian fermionic states, essential quantum simulation primitives -- transition amplitudes, overlaps, and arbitrary-weight number correlators -- can be efficiently approximated to additive error under free-fermionic dynamics. This tractability stems from an algebraic reduction that compresses exponentially large multiparticle interference into a single coefficient of a multivariate Pfaffian polynomial. Because these classical estimators match the intrinsic $O(1/\sqrt{K})$ statistical uncertainty of quantum hardware utilizing $K$ measurement shots, they constitute a practical benchmark. Building on this foundation, we construct an additive-error estimator for high-weight Wilson observables in the noninteracting quench of recent trapped-ion experiments, providing a rigorous classical benchmark. Extending this to quantum chemistry, we demonstrate that core overlap-based subroutines for antisymmetrized products of strongly orthogonal geminals admit exact Pfaffian reductions. Ultimately, these results sharpen the boundary of quantum advantage, establishing that the paired-electron scaffold is effectively dequantized and clarifying exactly where quantum resources are indispensable.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies a class of paired non-Gaussian fermionic states where quantum simulation primitives like transition amplitudes and correlators can be efficiently approximated using classical algorithms based on Pfaffian polynomials, matching the statistical accuracy of quantum hardware. This effectively "dequantizes" certain paired-electron systems, sharpening the boundary of quantum advantage.
AI.motivation: To precisely delineate the computational boundary between classically tractable fermionic systems and those requiring quantum advantage, specifically investigating whether non-Gaussian "magic" inputs always lead to intractable complexity in fermionic quantum simulation.
AI.method: The authors employ rigorous mathematical bounds and numerical evidence to show that paired non-Gaussian fermionic states admit an algebraic reduction compressing exponential multiparticle interference into a single coefficient of a multivariate Pfaffian polynomial, enabling efficient classical approximation of transition amplitudes, overlaps, and arbitrary-weight number correlators under free-fermionic dynamics.
AI.result: Classical estimators achieve additive error matching the intrinsic O(1/√K) statistical uncertainty of quantum hardware using K measurement shots, serving as practical benchmarks. The framework applies to high-weight Wilson observables in trapped-ion experiments and core overlap subroutines for antisymmetrized products of strongly orthogonal geminals in quantum chemistry.
AI.conclusion: The paired-electron scaffold is effectively dequantized, sharply refining the boundary of quantum advantage by showing that quantum resources are not indispensable for this class of fermionic systems.

title: Gouy phase engineering of self-splitting quantum correlations
summary: In this work, we demonstrate the effect of self-splitting spatial quantum correlations induced by Gouy phase engineering. In the process of spontaneous parametric down conversion the pump beam is structured with a mode superposition that produces a dynamical splitting and recombination of the light beam. This structure is transferred to the quantum correlations between signal and idler photons. As a result the joint two-photon probability distribution propagates like a self-splitting and recombining light beam, implementing a Mach-Zehnder-like interferometer. We observe heralded single-photon interference and two-photon NOON state interference. These results open new avenues for applications in quantum metrology.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that structuring the pump beam with mode superpositions in spontaneous parametric down conversion creates self-splitting spatial quantum correlations via Gouy phase engineering, leading to a Mach-Zehnder-like interferometer for heralded single-photon and two-photon NOON state interference, with applications in quantum metrology.
AI.motivation: To explore how Gouy phase engineering can induce self-splitting spatial quantum correlations in spontaneous parametric down conversion, enabling a novel interferometric scheme without physical beam splitters.
AI.method: The pump beam in spontaneous parametric down conversion is structured with a mode superposition that causes dynamical splitting and recombination of the light beam. This structure is transferred to the quantum correlations between signal and idler photons, producing a self-splitting and recombining joint two-photon probability distribution.
AI.result: Observation of heralded single-photon interference and two-photon NOON state interference, demonstrating that the joint two-photon probability distribution propagates like a self-splitting and recombining light beam, effectively implementing a Mach-Zehnder-like interferometer.
AI.conclusion: The results open new avenues for applications in quantum metrology by enabling interferometric schemes based on structured quantum correlations rather than physical beam splitters.

title: Quantum Feature Selection with Higher-Order Binary Optimization on Trapped-Ion Hardware
summary: We present a quantum feature-selection framework based on a higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) formulation that explicitly incorporates multivariate dependencies beyond standard quadratic encodings. In contrast to QUBO-based approaches, the proposed model includes one-, two-, and three-body interaction terms derived from mutual-information measures, enabling the objective function to capture feature relevance, pairwise redundancy, and higher-order statistical structure within a unified energy model. To suppress trivial all-selected solutions, we further include structured linear penalties that promote sparsity while preserving informative variables. The resulting HUBO instances are optimized with digitized counterdiabatic quantum optimization on IonQ Forte and compared against noiseless quantum simulation as well as two classical dimensionality-reduction baselines: SelectKBest based on mutual information and principal component analysis (PCA). We evaluate the proposed workflow on two benchmark classification datasets, namely the Gallstone dataset and the Spambase dataset, and analyze both predictive performance and selected-subset structure. The results show good qualitative agreement between hardware executions and noiseless simulations, supporting the feasibility of implementing higher-order feature-selection Hamiltonians on current trapped-ion processors. In addition, the quantum approach yields competitive classification performance while producing compact and informative feature subsets, highlighting the potential of higher-order quantum optimization for machine-learning preprocessing tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a quantum feature-selection framework using higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) that includes multivariate dependencies up to three-body interactions. The method is implemented on IonQ Forte, evaluated on Gallstone and Spambase datasets, and shows competitive performance with classical baselines while demonstrating feasibility on current trapped-ion processors.
AI.motivation: Existing QUBO-based quantum feature selection only captures pairwise interactions, missing higher-order statistical dependencies among features. The authors aim to incorporate multivariate dependencies beyond quadratic encodings to improve feature selection quality.
AI.method: A HUBO formulation with one-, two-, and three-body interaction terms derived from mutual-information measures, plus structured linear penalties for sparsity. Optimized via digitized counterdiabatic quantum optimization on IonQ Forte, compared against noiseless quantum simulation, SelectKBest (mutual information), and PCA.
AI.result: Good qualitative agreement between hardware executions and noiseless simulations. Competitive classification performance with compact and informative feature subsets on Gallstone and Spambase benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: Higher-order quantum optimization for feature selection is feasible on current trapped-ion processors and shows potential for machine-learning preprocessing tasks, motivating further exploration of multivariate quantum feature selection.

title: Fluctuations of path-dependent thermodynamic quantities in open quantum systems via two-point system-only measurements
summary: We propose a method to evaluate general thermodynamic fluctuations in open quantum systems, based on performing a two-point measurement scheme on the system using dynamics-dependent thermodynamic observables. Our approach allows one to obtain exact equalities for fluctuations of path-dependent thermodynamic quantities such as work and heat, and to isolate correction factors to Jarzynski's equality, requiring only access to the system degrees of freedom. This framework is flexible and can be applied to the limiting case of closed systems, recovering previous, yet seemingly contradictory, results from the literature. Moreover, the formalism admits a straightforward extension to strongly coupled open quantum systems. We investigate the effect of specific dynamical classes on the fluctuation relations, and show that the pure decoherence case is particularly special, as it deterministically does not contain any heat contribution and thus constitutes a class of open system dynamics for which the Jarzynski equality for work fluctuations is identically true at any coupling strength. Finally, we look explicitly at the shape and size of the correction factors to Jarzynski's equality for a qubit undergoing phase covariant dynamics, both in the weakly-coupled regime and in the deep non-Markovian regime.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to evaluate thermodynamic fluctuations in open quantum systems using a two-point measurement scheme with dynamics-dependent observables, enabling exact equalities for work and heat, correction factors to Jarzynski's equality, and extension to strongly coupled systems. Pure decoherence is identified as a special case where Jarzynski's equality holds identically at any coupling strength.
AI.motivation: To develop a general framework for evaluating thermodynamic fluctuations in open quantum systems that can handle path-dependent quantities like work and heat, resolve contradictions in prior literature, and extend to strongly coupled regimes.
AI.method: The method employs a two-point measurement scheme on the system using dynamics-dependent thermodynamic observables, allowing exact equalities for fluctuations of work and heat and isolation of correction factors to Jarzynski's equality using only system degrees of freedom.
AI.result: The framework recovers previous results in the closed-system limit, extends naturally to strongly coupled open quantum systems, identifies pure decoherence as a special dynamical class with no heat contribution where Jarzynski's equality holds exactly at any coupling, and characterizes correction factors for a qubit under phase covariant dynamics in weak and deep non-Markovian regimes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a flexible and unified approach to thermodynamic fluctuation relations in open quantum systems, revealing that certain dynamical classes (like pure decoherence) preserve Jarzynski's equality intact even under strong coupling.

title: Protein folding on a 64 qubit trapped-ion hardware via counterdiabatic quantum optimization
summary: We report the largest trapped-ion hardware demonstration of lattice protein-folding optimization to date, using bias-field digitized counterdiabatic quantum optimization (BF-DCQO) on a fully connected 64-qubit Barium development system similar to the forthcoming IonQ Tempo line. Six peptide sequences with 14-16 amino-acid residues are encoded using a coarse-grained tetrahedral lattice model, yielding higher-order spin-glass Hamiltonians with long-range interactions involving up to five-body terms and mapped to 46-61 qubits. The resulting instances are demanding for near-term quantum hardware because low-energy configurations must satisfy backbone-geometry constraints while optimizing dense residue-contact interactions. BF-DCQO uses a non-variational bias-feedback mechanism, where low-energy samples from each round define longitudinal fields that guide subsequent quantum evolutions. Across the studied instances, BF-DCQO shifts raw sampled energy distributions toward lower energies than uniform random sampling, with the strongest improvements appearing in residue-contact variables. To preserve this signal, we introduce a consensus-based post-processing pipeline that combines quantum-learned contact information with feasible backbone geometries. The resulting hybrid workflow reaches the classical reference energy in multiple instances and improves over the corresponding random-seeded pipeline. These results show that BF-DCQO can generate structured samples for dense protein-folding Hamiltonians at previously unexplored trapped-ion scales.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates the largest trapped-ion hardware implementation of lattice protein-folding optimization using BF-DCQO on a 64-qubit Barium system. Six peptide sequences (14-16 amino acids) are encoded as higher-order spin-glass Hamiltonians (up to five-body terms) mapped to 46-61 qubits. BF-DCQO with bias-feedback mechanism shifts energy distributions toward lower energies, and a consensus-based post-processing pipeline combining quantum-learned contact information with feasible backbone geometries achieves classical reference energies in multiple instances.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that non-variational bias-field digitized counterdiabatic quantum optimization (BF-DCQO) can generate structured, low-energy samples for dense protein-folding Hamiltonians at previously unexplored trapped-ion hardware scales (up to 61 qubits), overcoming the challenge that low-energy configurations must satisfy backbone-geometry constraints while optimizing dense residue-contact interactions.
AI.method: BF-DCQO employs a non-variational bias-feedback mechanism where low-energy samples from each round define longitudinal fields guiding subsequent quantum evolutions. A consensus-based post-processing pipeline combines quantum-learned contact information with feasible backbone geometries to produce final candidate configurations.
AI.result: BF-DCQO shifts raw sampled energy distributions toward lower energies compared to uniform random sampling, with strongest improvements in residue-contact variables. The hybrid workflow reaches classical reference energy in multiple instances and improves over the corresponding random-seeded pipeline.
AI.conclusion: BF-DCQO can generate structured samples for dense protein-folding Hamiltonians at previously unexplored trapped-ion scales, demonstrating a viable path for near-term quantum optimization of complex spin-glass problems with higher-order interactions.

title: MLMC-qDRIFT: Multilevel Variance Reduction for Randomized Quantum Hamiltonian Simulation
summary:
Simulating quantum dynamics is one of the central applications of quantum computing. For Hamiltonians written as a sum of many terms, deterministic Trotter--Suzuki product formulas can require applying a large number of term-wise evolutions at each time step, leading to high circuit costs for large or dense systems. Randomized methods such as qDRIFT offer an alternative: each step samples only one Hamiltonian term, giving a circuit depth with no explicit dependence on the number of terms. However, when qDRIFT is used for observable estimation, high precision requires many independent random circuit realizations, resulting in a total gate complexity that scales as $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3})$.
  We introduce a multilevel Monte Carlo framework for qDRIFT that reduces this sampling overhead. The method constructs a hierarchy of qDRIFT estimators with increasing circuit depths and couples adjacent levels by sharing their random Hamiltonian-term samples. This coupling makes the variance of the level differences decay with depth, allowing most samples to be taken on cheaper, coarse circuits and only a few on expensive, fine circuits. We prove that the resulting MLMC-qDRIFT estimator reduces the total gate complexity for fixed-precision observable estimation from the standard qDRIFT scaling $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-3})$ to $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-2}\log^2(1/\varepsilon))$, while preserving qDRIFT's lack of explicit dependence on the number of Hamiltonian terms. Numerical experiments for spin-chain dynamics confirm the predicted variance decay and demonstrate the practical gate-count savings of the multilevel construction.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a multilevel Monte Carlo (MLMC) framework for qDRIFT that reduces the gate complexity for observable estimation from O(ε⁻³) to O(ε⁻² log²(1/ε)), while maintaining qDRIFT's independence from the number of Hamiltonian terms.
AI.motivation: Standard qDRIFT requires O(ε⁻³) gate complexity for observable estimation due to needing many independent random circuit realizations for high precision. The goal is to reduce this sampling overhead.
AI.method: The authors propose a multilevel Monte Carlo (MLMC) framework for qDRIFT that constructs a hierarchy of estimators with increasing circuit depths. Adjacent levels are coupled by sharing random Hamiltonian-term samples, which makes variance decay with depth. Most samples are taken on cheaper coarse circuits, with only a few on expensive fine circuits.
AI.result: The MLMC-qDRIFT estimator reduces total gate complexity from O(ε⁻³) to O(ε⁻² log²(1/ε)), while preserving qDRIFT's lack of explicit dependence on the number of Hamiltonian terms.
AI.conclusion: Numerical experiments for spin-chain dynamics confirm the predicted variance decay and demonstrate practical gate-count savings of the multilevel construction.

title: Convex combinations of bosonic pure-loss channels
summary: The pure-loss channel is a fundamental model for describing noise in bosonic quantum platforms. It is characterised by a single parameter, the transmissivity, which quantifies the fraction of the input energy that reaches the output of the channel. In realistic scenarios, however, such as free-space quantum communication, the transmissivity is not fixed but fluctuates from one channel use to another. In this setting, the overall channel is effectively described as a convex combination of pure-loss channels, known as a fading channel. Despite its practical relevance, the quantum Shannon theory of the fading channel has remained largely unexplored. Here, we address this gap, specifically investigating degradability, anti-degradability, entanglement breakingness, and capacities of the fading channel. Of particular relevance to practical quantum-internet applications, we prove that entanglement distribution and quantum key distribution can always be achieved at a strictly positive rate over any fading channel, no matter how noisy it is or how strongly the transmissivity fluctuates, provided the channel is not completely noisy. Moreover, we prove that thermal states, which are optimal for a broad class of static bosonic Gaussian channels, fail to achieve the entanglement-assisted classical capacity of fading channels: non-Gaussian Fock-diagonal states strictly outperform all Gaussian encodings. Most strikingly, we identify regimes where the coherent information of thermal inputs vanishes, while optimized non-Gaussian states achieve strictly positive values, thereby activating the channel for quantum communication. For a paradigmatic binary fading model we establish this result analytically, deriving the exact capacity-achieving state in closed form. For general fading distributions, we design an iterative variational algorithm to optimize the coherent and mutual information.

AI.tldr: This paper studies fading quantum channels (convex combinations of pure-loss channels with fluctuating transmissivity), proving that entanglement distribution and QKD are always possible at positive rates over any non-completely-noisy fading channel, that non-Gaussian Fock-diagonal states outperform Gaussian ones for entanglement-assisted classical capacity, and that optimized non-Gaussian states can activate quantum communication where thermal inputs yield zero coherent information.
AI.motivation: Despite practical relevance in free-space quantum communication where transmissivity fluctuates, the quantum Shannon theory of fading channels (convex combinations of pure-loss channels) has remained largely unexplored.
AI.method: The study employs quantum Shannon theory analysis of degradability, anti-degradability, and capacities; analytical derivation for binary fading model; and an iterative variational algorithm for general fading distributions to optimize coherent and mutual information.
AI.result: (1) Entanglement distribution and QKD are always achievable at positive rates over any fading channel that is not completely noisy. (2) Thermal states fail to achieve the entanglement-assisted classical capacity; non-Gaussian Fock-diagonal states strictly outperform Gaussian encodings. (3) Regimes exist where thermal inputs yield zero coherent information but optimized non-Gaussian states achieve positive values, activating quantum communication.
AI.conclusion: Fading channels, despite noise and transmissivity fluctuations, can always support positive-rate entanglement distribution and QKD; non-Gaussian states are strictly superior to Gaussian encodings for certain capacities, and can activate quantum communication where thermal states fail.

title: Strict Hierarchy for Quantum Channel Certification to Unitary
summary:
We consider the problem of quantum channel certification to unitary, where one is given access to an unknown $d$-dimensional channel $\mathcal{E}$, and wants to test whether $\mathcal{E}$ is equal to a target unitary channel or is $\varepsilon$-far from it in the diamond norm. We present optimal quantum algorithms for this problem, settling the query complexities in three access models with increasing power. Specifically, we show that:
  (i) $Θ(d/\varepsilon^2)$ queries suffice for incoherent access model, matching the lower bound due to Fawzi, Flammarion, Garivier, and Oufkir (COLT 2023).
  (ii) $Θ(d/\varepsilon)$ queries suffice for coherent access model, matching the lower bound due to Regev and Schiff (ICALP 2008).
  (iii) $Θ(\sqrt{d}/\varepsilon)$ queries suffice for source-code access model, matching the lower bound due to Jeon and Oh (npj Quantum Inf. 2026).
  This demonstrates a strict hierarchy of complexities for quantum channel certification to unitary across various access models.

AI.tldr: This paper presents optimal quantum algorithms for certifying whether an unknown quantum channel equals a target unitary channel or is ε-far from it, establishing tight query complexities in three access models: incoherent (Θ(d/ε²)), coherent (Θ(d/ε)), and source-code (Θ(√d/ε)).
AI.motivation: To determine the minimal number of queries needed to certify a quantum channel against a target unitary, settling the query complexities across different access models with matching lower bounds.
AI.method: The authors develop optimal quantum algorithms for channel certification in three access models (incoherent, coherent, source-code) and prove matching lower bounds using techniques from prior work (Fawzi et al., Regev & Schiff, Jeon & Oh).
AI.result: Query complexities: incoherent Θ(d/ε²), coherent Θ(d/ε), source-code Θ(√d/ε), demonstrating a strict hierarchy of complexities.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchy shows that more powerful access models (coherent and source-code) enable exponentially better query efficiency for quantum channel certification to unitary.

title: Cavity-mediated coherence protection and one-axis twisting for spins in solids
comment: RF and CL contributed equally to this work
summary: Long-range interactions between emitters give rise to collective phenomena, including superradiance, spin squeezing, and coherence protection, that are important to both fundamental physics and quantum technologies. Despite progress in cold atoms, coherent cavity-mediated all-to-all interactions have not yet been realized in a solid-state ensemble. Here we demonstrate such interactions in a $^{171}$Yb$^{3+}$:CaWO$_4$ crystal coupled to a microwave resonator, observing superradiant emission on resonance and unitary one-axis twisting dynamics in the dispersive regime. The same interaction also opens a many-body energy gap that suppresses inhomogeneous dephasing, extending the ensemble Ramsey coherence time from tens of microseconds to milliseconds without decoupling pulses. These results establish a solid-state platform for collective many-body physics with direct implications for quantum technologies. Specifically, the observed one-axis twisting dynamics opens a path towards spin squeezing for entanglement-enhanced quantum metrology, and the extended coherence due to gap-protection is relevant for both microwave photon storage and precision measurement.

AI.tldr: All-to-all cavity-mediated interactions are demonstrated in a solid-state ensemble (171Yb3+:CaWO4 crystal coupled to a microwave resonator), enabling superradiance, one-axis twisting dynamics, and many-body gap protection that extends Ramsey coherence from microseconds to milliseconds, establishing a platform for quantum metrology and many-body physics.
AI.motivation: To realize coherent cavity-mediated all-to-all interactions in a solid-state ensemble, which has been achieved in cold atoms but not in solid-state systems, to enable collective many-body phenomena for fundamental physics and quantum technologies.
AI.method: Coupling a 171Yb3+:CaWO4 crystal to a microwave resonator, operating both on resonance (for superradiance) and in the dispersive regime (for one-axis twisting dynamics and gap protection).
AI.result: Demonstration of superradiant emission on resonance, unitary one-axis twisting dynamics in the dispersive regime, and a many-body energy gap that suppresses inhomogeneous dephasing, extending Ramsey coherence time from tens of microseconds to milliseconds without decoupling pulses.
AI.conclusion: This solid-state platform enables collective many-body physics with direct quantum technology applications: one-axis twisting for spin squeezing in entanglement-enhanced quantum metrology, and gap-protected coherence for microwave photon storage and precision measurement.

title: Digital Simulation of Non-Hermitian Knotted Bands on Quantum Hardware
summary: Knots and links represent a fundamental motif of non-local connectivity that permeates the physical sciences from string theory to protein folds. While spectral braiding has been explored in two-band non-Hermitian models across various platforms, its direct simulation and characterization on programmable quantum hardware, particularly beyond two strands, remains a formidable challenge due to the limitations of variational optimization in these systems. Here, we introduce a family of non-Hermitian multi-band twister models and implement a non-variational protocol to characterize their complex braided band structures on a programmable superconducting quantum processor. By mapping the winding of eigenstates to the spectral topology, we devise an efficient measurement strategy that extracts braid information, including braid words and knot invariants like the Alexander and Jones polynomials, without requiring full spectral tomography or repeated optimization. We experimentally demonstrate the reconstruction of complicated knots and links such as the Hopf chain and Solomon's knot. Our approach provides a general framework for investigating exotic non-Hermitian topology on near-term quantum devices, opening a route to simulate more sophisticated topological structures in knot theory.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces non-Hermitian multi-band twister models and a non-variational protocol to characterize braided band structures on a superconducting quantum processor, enabling reconstruction of knots and links (e.g., Hopf chain, Solomon's knot) via extracted braid words and knot invariants without full spectral tomography.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of variational optimization in simulating spectral braiding beyond two strands on programmable quantum hardware, enabling direct characterization of complex non-Hermitian braided band structures.
AI.method: A family of non-Hermitian multi-band twister models is introduced, along with a non-variational protocol that maps eigenstate winding to spectral topology. Efficient measurement strategies extract braid words and knot invariants (Alexander and Jones polynomials) without full spectral tomography or repeated optimization, implemented on a programmable superconducting quantum processor.
AI.result: Experimental reconstruction of complicated knots and links, including the Hopf chain and Solomon's knot, demonstrating successful extraction of braid information and knot invariants.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a general framework for investigating exotic non-Hermitian topology on near-term quantum devices, opening routes to simulate more sophisticated topological structures in knot theory.

title: Rethinking Nonlocality: Locality, Counterfactuals, and the EPR-Bell Argument
summary: The widespread claim that violations of Bell inequalities establish the nonlocality of nature is critically reexamined. It is argued that this conclusion is not logically compelled by either the Einstein--Podolsky--Rosen (EPR) argument or Bell's theorem. The analysis highlights the central role of counterfactual reasoning -- the assumption that outcomes of unperformed measurements possess definite values -- in deriving Bell inequalities. It is shown that these inequalities follow not from locality alone, but from the conjunction of locality with a global assignment of values across incompatible measurement contexts. Their experimental violation therefore signals the impossibility of such a global assignment, i.e.\ contextuality, rather than necessarily implying nonlocal causation. This interpretation aligns with Bohr's emphasis on the contextual character of physical quantities and is naturally formulated within modern sheaf-theoretic approaches to contextuality.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that violations of Bell inequalities demonstrate contextuality rather than nonlocality, as the inequalities follow from the conjunction of locality with global value assignments across incompatible measurement contexts.
AI.motivation: To critically reexamine the widespread claim that Bell inequality violations establish the nonlocality of nature, and to offer an alternative interpretation based on contextuality.
AI.method: Logically reanalyzing the EPR argument and Bell's theorem, focusing on the role of counterfactual reasoning and the assumption of definite values for unperformed measurements. The analysis uses modern sheaf-theoretic frameworks for contextuality.
AI.result: Bell inequalities follow from locality plus global value assignments across incompatible measurement contexts, not from locality alone. Their violation thus indicates the impossibility of global assignment (contextuality), not nonlocal causation.
AI.conclusion: The nonlocality conclusion is not logically compelled by Bell's theorem; violations can be consistently interpreted as evidence of contextuality, aligning with Bohr's views and sheaf-theoretic approaches.

title: En Route to a Standard QMA1 vs. QCMA Oracle Separation
summary: We study the power of quantum witnesses under perfect completeness. We construct a classical oracle relative to which a language lies in $\mathsf{QMA}_1$ but not in $\mathsf{QCMA}$ when the $\mathsf{QCMA}$ verifier is only allowed polynomially many adaptive rounds and exponentially many parallel queries per round. Additionally, we derandomize the permutation-oracle separation of Fefferman and Kimmel, obtaining an in-place oracle separation between $\mathsf{QMA}_1$ and $\mathsf{QCMA}$. Furthermore, we focus on $\mathsf{QCMA}$ and $\mathsf{QMA}$ with an exponentially small gap, where we show a separation assuming the gap is fixed, but not when it may be arbitrarily small. Finally, we derive consequences for approximate ground-state preparation from sparse Hamiltonian oracle access, including a bounded-adaptivity frustration-free variant.

AI.tldr: This paper studies oracle separations between QMA₁ and QCMA, showing a classical oracle where a language is in QMA₁ but not in QCMA with polynomial adaptivity and exponential parallel queries, derandomizes a permutation-oracle separation, and examines separations under exponentially small gaps, with implications for Hamiltonian ground-state preparation.
AI.motivation: To understand the power of quantum witnesses (QMA) versus classical witnesses (QCMA) under perfect completeness, and to explore separations with bounded adaptivity and exponentially small promise gaps.
AI.method: Constructs a classical oracle separation via QMA₁ vs QCMA with polynomially many adaptive rounds and exponentially many parallel queries per round; derandomizes Fefferman and Kimmel's permutation-oracle separation into an in-place oracle separation; analyzes fixed vs arbitrarily small gap conditions.
AI.result: A classical oracle separates QMA₁ from QCMA under bounded adaptivity and exponential parallel queries; an in-place oracle separation is obtained via derandomization; a separation holds for fixed exponentially small gap but not for arbitrarily small gaps; consequences for approximate ground-state preparation from sparse Hamiltonian oracles (including bounded-adaptivity frustration-free case) are derived.
AI.conclusion: The results provide new oracle separations between quantum and classical witness classes under realistic constraints (bounded adaptivity, exponentially small gaps) and link these to practical problems in Hamiltonian ground-state preparation.

title: The most discriminable quantum states in the multicopy regime
summary: This work investigates which sets of quantum states give rise to the highest achievable success probability in minimum-error state discrimination if multiple copies of the unknown state are given. Specifically, we consider uniformly distributed ensembles of the form $\left\{\frac{1}{N},ρ_i^{\otimes k}\right\}_{i=1}^N$, where $N$ states in dimension $d$ are provided in $k$ identical copies, and derive universal limits in this scenario. For pure state ensembles, we prove that whenever $N$ is large enough to support a state $k$-design, these designs will exactly give rise to the maximally discriminable sets. We further show that when $N$ exceeds the size required for a $k$-design, mixed states can outperform all pure state ensembles. We also analyse the analogue classical discrimination problems, in which states are replaced by probability distributions. We recognise that the problem of most discriminable classical states in the multi-copy regime is in one-to-one correspondence to the concept of the multiplicative Bayes capacity of independent uses of classical channels, a concept that emerges naturally in the context of classical information leakage. This connection allows us to completely solve the classical analogue of our problem when $N\geq \binom{d + k - 1}{k}$, and to prove that quantum systems offer a quadratic advantage (in number of copies $k$) over classical ones. Curiously, we also show that this quantum advantage is strongly reduced when one is restricted to real quantum states. Finally, we introduce computational techniques to find sets of most discriminable ensembles, and to obtain rigorous universal upper bounds on the maximal success probability for multi-copy state discrimination in cases that are analytically intractable.

AI.tldr: This paper studies which quantum state ensembles maximize success probability in minimum-error state discrimination when multiple copies are available. Pure state k-designs are optimal for large N; mixed states can outperform pure ones beyond the k-design threshold. A classical analogue links to multiplicative Bayes capacity, and quantum systems show a quadratic advantage over classical, though this advantage diminishes for real quantum states.
AI.motivation: To understand which sets of quantum states yield the highest success probability in minimum-error discrimination when multiple copies of an unknown state are provided, and to compare quantum vs. classical discrimination capabilities.
AI.method: The authors use uniform ensembles of k-copy quantum states, prove that pure state k-designs are optimal when N is large enough, analyze mixed states beyond that threshold, connect the classical analogue to multiplicative Bayes capacity, and develop computational techniques for intractable cases.
AI.result: Pure state k-designs maximize discrimination success for large N; mixed states can outperform all pure ensembles when N exceeds k-design size; classical analogue solved for N ≥ binomial(d+k-1, k); quantum systems offer a quadratic advantage in copies over classical; this advantage is strongly reduced for real quantum states.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes fundamental limits on multi-copy state discrimination, reveals a quadratic quantum advantage over classical discrimination, and provides computational tools for analyzing otherwise intractable regimes.

title: Simulating dynamics of RLC circuits with a quantum differential-algebraic equations solver
summary: We introduce a quantum algorithm for simulating the dynamics of electrical circuits consisting of resistors, inductors and capacitors (aka RLC circuits) along with power sources. Given oracle access to the connectivity of the circuit and values of the electrical elements, our algorithm prepares a quantum state that encodes voltages and current values either at a specified time or the history of their evolution over a time-interval. For an RLC circuit with $N$ components, our algorithm runs in time $\textsf{polylog}(N)$ under mild assumptions on the connectivity of the circuit and values of its components. This provides an exponential speed-up over classical algorithms that take $\textsf{poly}(N)$ time in the worst-case. Our algorithm can be used to estimate energy across a set of components or dissipated power in $\textsf{polylog}(N)$ time, a problem that we prove is BQP-hard and therefore unlikely to be efficiently solved by classical algorithms. The main challenge in simulating the dynamics of RLC circuits is that they are governed by differential-algebraic equations (DAEs), a coupled system of differential equations with hidden algebraic constraints. Consequentially, existing quantum algorithms for ordinary differential equations cannot be directly utilized. We therefore develop a quantum DAE solver for simulating the time-evolution of linear DAEs. For RLC circuits, we employ modified nodal analysis to create a system of DAEs compatible with our quantum algorithm. We establish BQP-hardness by demonstrating that any network of classical harmonic oscillators, for which an energy-estimation problem is known to be BQP-hard, is a special case of an LC circuit. Our work gives theoretical evidence of quantum advantage in simulating RLC circuits and we expect that our quantum DAE solver will find broader use in the simulation of dynamical systems.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a quantum algorithm for simulating RLC circuit dynamics that achieves exponential speedup (polylog(N) vs poly(N)) over classical methods, by developing a quantum DAE solver to handle the differential-algebraic equations governing such circuits, and proves BQP-hardness for energy estimation in these systems.
AI.motivation: Simulating RLC circuits classically requires poly(N) time, and energy estimation in such circuits is BQP-hard, suggesting classical algorithms are fundamentally limited. The authors aim to achieve quantum advantage by developing an exponentially faster quantum algorithm for this problem.
AI.method: The authors develop a quantum DAE solver tailored for linear differential-algebraic equations, which arise from modified nodal analysis of RLC circuits. The algorithm assumes oracle access to circuit connectivity and component values, and prepares quantum states encoding voltages and currents at specified times or over time intervals.
AI.result: The quantum algorithm runs in polylog(N) time under mild assumptions, offering exponential speedup over classical poly(N) algorithms. Energy estimation and dissipated power computation are also achieved in polylog(N) time. BQP-hardness is established by showing that classical harmonic oscillator networks (known to be BQP-hard) are a special case of LC circuits.
AI.conclusion: The work provides theoretical evidence of quantum advantage in simulating RLC circuits. The quantum DAE solver developed is expected to have broader applications beyond circuits to other dynamical systems governed by differential-algebraic equations.
